questions.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration with Stop Reasons
questions.text: Your organization is building a multi-agent research system where a supervisor agent delegates tasks to specialized research agents (market analysis, competitive intelligence, regulatory compliance). Each agent must report back with confidence scores and cite sources. The supervisor needs to make routing decisions based on whether child agents have sufficient information or should continue research. Which Claude API feature should you implement to enable the supervisor to detect when a research agent has gathered enough data and stop gracefully without wasting tokens?

options.A: Memory tool to persist research state between conversation turns
options.B: Stop reasons and fallback to detect completion signals and route to alternative agents if needed
options.C: Prompt caching to store research templates across all agents
options.D: Batch processing to queue all research requests asynchronously

questions.explanation: Stop reasons enable the supervisor to detect when a child agent reaches a natural stopping point (e.g., max_tokens, end_turn, tool_use) and decide whether to continue or route to another agent. Fallback allows graceful degradation if an agent refuses a task. This is core orchestration logic in agentic architectures.

optionExplanations.C: Prompt caching optimizes cost and latency for repeated prompts but does not help detect completion signals or manage agent handoffs.
optionExplanations.D: Batch processing is for asynchronous bulk requests, not for real-time multi-agent coordination and routing decisions.
optionExplanations.A: Memory tool stores knowledge across conversations but is not designed to signal task completion or orchestrate agent transitions.
optionExplanations.B: Stop reasons enable the supervisor to detect when a child agent reaches a natural stopping point (e.g., max_tokens, end_turn, tool_use) and decide whether to continue or route to another agent. Fallback allows graceful degradation if an agent refuses a task. This is core orchestration logic in agentic architectures.

questions.title: MCP Server Integration for Enterprise Data
questions.text: Your enterprise has a custom financial data warehouse that must remain behind your firewall. You want to expose this data securely to Claude via the Claude API without rewriting your entire integration layer. You need Claude to query the warehouse, retrieve structured financial reports, and ground its analysis in actual records. According to the Model Context Protocol, which three core primitives should you expose through your MCP server implementation to support this use case?

options.A: Tools for querying, resources for report templates, and prompts for financial analysis contexts
options.B: Thinking mode, structured outputs, and token counting
options.C: Web search, code execution, and the Files API
options.D: Skills, embeddings, and batch operations

questions.explanation: MCP's three core primitives are tools (for actions like database queries), resources (for static or semi-static content like report templates and financial data), and prompts (for reusable prompt patterns and contexts specific to financial analysis). This design allows Claude to interact with external services securely.

optionExplanations.D: Skills and embeddings are not part of MCP's core primitives. Batch operations are a Claude API feature, not an MCP primitive.
optionExplanations.C: Web search and code execution are Claude tools, not MCP primitives. Files API is a Claude feature, not part of MCP.
optionExplanations.B: Thinking mode, structured outputs, and token counting are Claude model capabilities, not MCP primitives.
optionExplanations.A: MCP's three core primitives are tools (for actions like database queries), resources (for static or semi-static content like report templates and financial data), and prompts (for reusable prompt patterns and contexts specific to financial analysis). This design allows Claude to interact with external services securely.

questions.title: Claude Code Skill Configuration for Reusability
questions.text: Your team is building a set of reusable Claude Code Skills for automated data processing tasks: one for CSV ingestion, one for data validation, and one for report generation. You want these Skills to be applied automatically by Claude when relevant, shared across team members, and easily updated without breaking existing workflows. Based on the Claude Partner Network learning path, what is the primary purpose of organizing these as Skills in Claude Code rather than as separate agent implementations?

options.A: Skills allow Claude to automatically discover and apply the right instructions at the right time without explicit routing logic
options.B: Skills reduce the total number of API calls needed by batching all operations together
options.C: Skills eliminate the need for context management by storing all instructions externally
options.D: Skills ensure that all team members run on the same Claude model version

questions.explanation: Skills are reusable markdown instructions that Claude automatically applies to the right tasks at the right time. They enable progressive disclosure of context, meaning Claude selects relevant Skills based on the task, avoiding token waste and improving orchestration efficiency.

optionExplanations.B: Skills do not batch API calls; they organize instructions for automatic selection and reuse within a session.
optionExplanations.D: Model versioning is controlled via the API, not by Skills organization.
optionExplanations.C: Skills still require context management; they optimize it through selective, task-aware instruction loading rather than eliminating it.
optionExplanations.A: Skills are reusable markdown instructions that Claude automatically applies to the right tasks at the right time. They enable progressive disclosure of context, meaning Claude selects relevant Skills based on the task, avoiding token waste and improving orchestration efficiency.

questions.title: Structured Extraction with Schema Conformance
questions.text: A legal tech company needs to extract contract terms (party names, effective dates, renewal clauses, liability caps) from thousands of uploaded PDF documents. The extracted data must conform exactly to a predefined JSON schema to feed downstream contract management systems. Manual validation of extraction errors is expensive. Which Claude API feature combination should you use to guarantee that Claude's output matches your schema and reduce downstream validation overhead?

options.A: Structured outputs using strict tool use to guarantee schema conformance and JSON validation
options.B: Prompt caching with streaming messages to optimize latency on repeated document types
options.C: Citations with PDF support to ground each extracted field in source text
options.D: Batch processing with effort parameter to control thoroughness of extraction

questions.explanation: Structured outputs using strict tool use guarantees that Claude's responses conform to your JSON schema, with validation enforced at the API level. This eliminates the need for post-processing validation and ensures downstream systems receive only valid data.

optionExplanations.C: Citations ground outputs in source text but do not guarantee schema conformance or prevent malformed JSON.
optionExplanations.B: Prompt caching and streaming optimize cost and latency but do not enforce schema conformance.
optionExplanations.D: Batch processing and effort parameter control cost and reasoning depth, not schema validation.
optionExplanations.A: Structured outputs using strict tool use guarantees that Claude's responses conform to your JSON schema, with validation enforced at the API level. This eliminates the need for post-processing validation and ensures downstream systems receive only valid data.

questions.title: Long-Running Agent Context Efficiency
questions.text: You are building a customer support agent that handles multi-turn conversations spanning hours or days. The agent accumulates conversation history, tool results from ticket systems, and knowledge base excerpts. Over time, the context window approaches the 1M token limit. You need the agent to remain reliable, efficient, and responsive without manually truncating conversation history. Which context management features should you combine to handle this scenario?

options.A: Memory tool to offload all historical context to persistent storage
options.B: Batch processing to defer long conversations to asynchronous jobs
options.C: Prompt caching alone with a 1-hour cache duration on all background knowledge
options.D: Context compaction and context editing to automatically summarize earlier conversation and drop stale tool results as needed

questions.explanation: Context compaction automatically summarizes earlier conversation when approaching token limits, and context editing allows selective removal of stale tool results and management of non-essential context. Together they keep long-running sessions efficient and reliable without manual intervention.

optionExplanations.C: Prompt caching optimizes cost for repeated prompts but does not address the accumulation of conversation history and tool results over time.
optionExplanations.B: Batch processing is for asynchronous bulk processing, not for managing live, interactive multi-turn conversations.
optionExplanations.A: Memory tool stores knowledge across separate conversations but does not actively trim context within a single long-running session.
optionExplanations.D: Context compaction automatically summarizes earlier conversation when approaching token limits, and context editing allows selective removal of stale tool results and management of non-essential context. Together they keep long-running sessions efficient and reliable without manual intervention.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration: Tool Conflict Resolution
questions.text: You're building a multi-agent research system where three agents (literature search, data analyst, and report writer) operate in sequence. The literature search agent calls a web_search tool, returning 15 results. The data analyst then needs to fetch full content from 3–5 of those results using web_fetch. However, your token budget for the entire workflow is 50,000 tokens, and you're currently burning 8,000 tokens on redundant tool context being passed to each agent. The report writer often requests the data analyst re-fetch sources that were already fetched earlier in the workflow. What is the BEST approach to reduce token waste while maintaining agent autonomy?

options.A: Use prompt caching on the tool definitions and search results, so that repeated references to the same sources across agent calls reuse cached tokens rather than reprocessing them.
options.B: Restructure the workflow so that all three agents run in parallel rather than sequentially, reducing the total number of tool invocations.
options.C: Remove web_fetch from the data analyst's available tools and instead have it only request raw URLs; the report writer filters which URLs to actually fetch.
options.D: Implement a shared tool context cache that all three agents reference, removing tool definitions from individual agent prompts and relying on a central registry.

questions.explanation: Prompt caching directly addresses the redundancy: tool definitions and earlier search results are cached for 5 minutes, so when the report writer requests re-fetching or the data analyst references prior results, those tokens are charged at the cache read rate (90% discount), not reprocessed. This preserves agent autonomy and workflow flexibility while eliminating the exact waste you identified.

optionExplanations.D: A shared tool context registry adds architectural complexity and requires synchronization logic between agents. It doesn't directly reduce token consumption—tool definitions still exist in the request payload and must be processed. Prompt caching is simpler and automatically manages token reuse.
optionExplanations.A: Prompt caching directly addresses the redundancy: tool definitions and earlier search results are cached for 5 minutes, so when the report writer requests re-fetching or the data analyst references prior results, those tokens are charged at the cache read rate (90% discount), not reprocessed. This preserves agent autonomy and workflow flexibility while eliminating the exact waste you identified.
optionExplanations.B: Parallel execution doesn't reduce token waste; in fact, it may increase it by forcing all three agents to be provided with the same context simultaneously. The core problem is that identical tool definitions and results are re-sent on each sequential call—parallelization doesn't solve that.
optionExplanations.C: This arbitrarily restricts the data analyst's capability and creates a false dependency on the report writer to decide what to fetch. It doesn't eliminate the redundant token cost; it just hides it in a different layer and reduces the system's flexibility.

questions.title: MCP Tool Definition Scope: Balancing Discovery vs. Context Overhead
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes 200 domain-specific tools for a financial services client (trade execution, risk calculation, market data retrieval, compliance checks, etc.). A single Claude session might use only 8–12 of these tools per interaction. Your monitoring shows that tool discovery and schema loading consume 2,100 tokens per session, and 34% of sessions fail to select any tools at all. The client's cost-per-interaction has risen 18% since deploying the full 200-tool catalog. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE way to optimize tool exposure without sacrificing Claude's ability to discover the right tools?

options.A: Implement tool search with regex-based filtering, allowing Claude to dynamically load only tools matching the current task context, rather than exposing all 200 tools upfront.
options.B: Create a thin tool abstraction layer with 20 high-level 'composite' tools that each wrap 8–10 related low-level tools, reducing the schema count Claude must evaluate.
options.C: Use prompt caching to store the full 200-tool schema once, so subsequent requests in the same conversation reuse the cached tool definitions and avoid re-computing schema tokens.
options.D: Partition the 200 tools into five separate MCP servers by domain, and have your orchestration layer decide which server to connect based on the user's stated intent before sending the request to Claude.

questions.explanation: Tool search with regex-based filtering lets Claude dynamically discover and load only tools relevant to the current task, eliminating the 2,100-token upfront cost of loading all 200 schemas. This is specifically designed for scaling to thousands of tools while maintaining Claude's autonomous discovery ability. It avoids the inflexibility of pre-partitioning (B) and the loss of precision from compositing (C).

optionExplanations.A: Tool search with regex-based filtering lets Claude dynamically discover and load only tools relevant to the current task, eliminating the 2,100-token upfront cost of loading all 200 schemas. This is specifically designed for scaling to thousands of tools while maintaining Claude's autonomous discovery ability.
optionExplanations.D: Pre-partitioning into separate MCP servers and using orchestration logic to pre-select which server to connect requires accurate upfront intent classification. If the client's intent is misclassified or spans multiple domains, Claude loses access to relevant tools. This trades flexibility for cost, which is a regression for 34% of sessions that don't use tools anyway.
optionExplanations.B: Compositing 200 tools into 20 abstractions reduces schema overhead but introduces a lossy abstraction layer. Claude loses visibility into the specific capabilities of low-level tools and may fail to recognize when a composite tool is appropriate for a nuanced request. This trades precision for cost.
optionExplanations.C: Prompt caching can help for repeated requests in the same conversation, but it doesn't address the core problem: the 2,100-token cost is incurred on the first request. For sessions where clients switch contexts or don't repeat tool patterns, caching provides little benefit. Tool search solves the problem at the source.

questions.title: Claude Code Skill Deployment: Activation Timing and Token Usage
questions.text: Your team has created three reusable Claude Code Skills for your internal platform: DataExplorer (exploratory analysis), ReportGenerator (formatted output), and QualityCheck (validation and error detection). You've configured the Skills in the API, but field telemetry shows that 22% of sessions load all three Skills upfront (9,400 tokens), while only 6% of those sessions actually invoke the QualityCheck skill. The cost per session has become unpredictable, and developers report confusion about when each Skill is automatically applied. What is the BEST approach to optimize Skill activation while maintaining Claude's ability to autonomously invoke them when needed?

options.A: Load only DataExplorer and ReportGenerator by default, and require users to explicitly request QualityCheck in their prompt when they need validation.
options.B: Use Skill progressive disclosure, configuring QualityCheck to load only when the conversation context indicates a validation task is required, rather than loading it upfront for all sessions.
options.C: Implement a two-phase approach: load DataExplorer only on the first request, then asynchronously load ReportGenerator and QualityCheck in the background while the first response streams to the user.
options.D: Combine Skills into a single composite Skill that bundles all three capabilities, reducing schema overhead and simplifying the decision logic for Claude.

questions.explanation: Skill progressive disclosure is designed exactly for this use case: Skills load dynamically based on conversation context and task type, rather than all upfront. This eliminates the 9,400-token cost for sessions that don't need all three, preserves Claude's autonomy to invoke QualityCheck when appropriate, and provides predictable token usage patterns.

optionExplanations.A: Requiring users to explicitly request QualityCheck breaks the goal of autonomous Skill invocation and shifts complexity to the user. If a user forgets to mention validation, they get an unvalidated result. This is error-prone and defeats the purpose of having Claude autonomously select the right Skill.
optionExplanations.B: Skill progressive disclosure is designed exactly for this use case: Skills load dynamically based on conversation context and task type, rather than all upfront. This eliminates the 9,400-token cost for sessions that don't need all three, preserves Claude's autonomy to invoke QualityCheck when appropriate, and provides predictable token usage patterns.
optionExplanations.D: Compositing three distinct Skills into one reduces schema overhead but forces Claude to always consider all three capabilities together, even when only one is relevant. It also makes it harder for developers to reason about when and why each capability is invoked. Progressive disclosure provides finer-grained control.
optionExplanations.C: Asynchronous background loading doesn't reduce the token cost—QualityCheck is still loaded on every session, just non-blocking. The 9,400-token burn still occurs; it's just not visible to the user. Progressive disclosure prevents the load entirely when not needed.

questions.title: Customer Support Classification: Balancing Precision and Fallback Behavior
questions.text: Your support ticket classification system uses Claude to assign incoming tickets to categories (billing, technical, returns, account security) and extract key metadata (priority, product ID, customer tier). Evaluation data shows 91% accuracy on the main model, but for the 9% edge cases (ambiguous tickets, mixed-issue tickets), the system currently defaults to a generic 'escalation' category, which requires manual human review and costs $2.40 per ticket. You've calculated that using a smaller, faster model on these edge cases would cost $0.08 per ticket but achieve only 78% accuracy. How should you configure the system to minimize total cost-to-resolution while maintaining data quality?

options.A: Implement a two-tier approach: run the main model on all tickets, flag uncertain predictions (confidence < 0.85) for the fallback model, and use structured outputs with JSON to capture both the primary classification and a secondary fallback classification for comparison.
options.B: Use the main model only, but add 3–5 few-shot examples of ambiguous tickets with clear human-reviewed classifications to the system prompt, improving edge-case accuracy without incurring fallback costs.
options.C: Use server-side fallback to retry refused or uncertain tickets on a smaller model. When the primary model cannot confidently classify a ticket, echo the refusal token to the fallback model to get a best-effort classification at reduced cost.
options.D: Route the bottom 9% of tickets (lowest-confidence predictions) to the smaller model post-hoc, and only escalate to humans if the smaller model also fails to classify confidently.

questions.explanation: This minimizes total cost by leveraging the smaller model's 78% accuracy where it provides value ($0.08 × 9% of tickets) and escalating only unresolved cases ($2.40 per remaining case). A 78% success rate on edge cases beats defaulting all 9% to expensive human escalation. Structured outputs alone (B) don't improve accuracy without new data. Few-shot examples (C) may help but don't reduce the cost of the 9% that remain genuinely ambiguous.

optionExplanations.C: Server-side fallback with refusal tokens is designed for handling model refusals (e.g., when a model declines to answer for policy reasons), not for managing edge cases or low-confidence predictions. Uncertainty is not the same as refusal. Using fallback here adds latency and cost without addressing the root problem: the smaller model still only achieves 78% accuracy on ambiguous tickets.
optionExplanations.A: Running both models on flagged tickets (confidence < 0.85) means you pay for both the main model and the fallback model on 9% of tickets. If the main model already processed the ticket with 91% accuracy, running a second model on edge cases doubles the cost for that batch and doesn't guarantee better results. The two-model approach is more expensive than sequential routing.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples may improve edge-case accuracy, but they don't reduce the 9% of genuinely ambiguous tickets that lack clear signal. Few-shot learning works best when the underlying patterns are learnable; tickets with conflicting signals or mixed issues often can't be disambiguated without additional context or human judgment.
optionExplanations.D: This minimizes total cost by leveraging the smaller model's 78% accuracy where it provides value ($0.08 × 9% of tickets) and escalating only unresolved cases ($2.40 per remaining case). A 78% success rate on edge cases beats defaulting all 9% to expensive human escalation.

questions.title: Context Window Saturation: Token Count vs. Response Quality Trade-off
questions.text: Your document analysis agent processes lengthy legal contracts (average 24,000 tokens) and extracts key clauses, obligations, and risk flags. Users often request follow-up analyses on the same contract (e.g., 'compare this clause to the prior draft'). You've instrumented token counting and noticed that after 6–7 follow-up interactions, the full contract is still in context, but latency has increased 34% and cost per interaction has risen by 28%. Your token budget allows for up to 800,000 tokens per conversation, and the contract occupies only 24,000 of that. What is the PRIMARY root cause, and which mitigation strategy is MOST appropriate?

options.A: The model generates progressively more verbose responses as it references more prior context. Implement an effort parameter set to low to constrain token generation and reduce output verbosity across follow-up turns.
options.B: Latency and cost increases are normal in long conversations and reflect the model's need to re-evaluate the full context on each turn. No optimization is needed; migrate to batch processing for these multi-turn workflows instead.
options.C: The increased latency and cost are due to the growing conversation history: each new request re-sends the full prior exchange history plus the original contract. Use context compaction to automatically summarize the conversation history when it exceeds 100,000 tokens, preserving only the essential prior analysis results.
options.D: The contract itself remains static in memory but is being re-processed and re-analyzed on each turn due to repeated scanning of the same clauses. Use prompt caching to cache the contract and prior analysis results, so follow-up requests reuse cached tokens at 90% cost reduction rather than reprocessing the full document.

questions.explanation: The 24,000-token contract and prior analysis results are static and repeated across follow-up requests. Prompt caching caches these elements for 5 minutes, so subsequent requests in the same conversation reuse them at 90% discount rather than charging full input tokens. This directly explains the 28% cost rise (full re-processing) and eliminates it. Compaction (A) targets conversation history growth, not static content reuse. Effort parameter (B) controls generation length, not input token cost.

optionExplanations.C: Compaction summarizes earlier conversation history when approaching token limits, but the root problem is that the static contract (24,000 tokens) is being fully re-sent and processed on each follow-up request, not that the conversation history is bloating. Compaction would help if conversation history were the bottleneck, but in this scenario the contract is the largest static block.
optionExplanations.A: The effort parameter controls how much computation Claude applies to reasoning and response generation, not input token cost. More verbose responses increase *output* tokens, not *input* token cost. Since the contract is re-sent each turn as input, the effort parameter doesn't address the 28% cost rise from reprocessing the contract.
optionExplanations.D: The 24,000-token contract and prior analysis results are static and repeated across follow-up requests. Prompt caching caches these elements for 5 minutes, so subsequent requests in the same conversation reuse them at 90% discount rather than charging full input tokens. This directly explains the 28% cost rise (full re-processing) and eliminates it.
optionExplanations.B: Batch processing is designed for asynchronous, high-volume request processing, not for interactive, stateful multi-turn analysis where users expect real-time responses. Batch processing would increase latency (the opposite of the goal) and doesn't address the root cause of the cost rise.

questions.title: Agent Skill Activation: Progressive Disclosure vs. Early Availability
questions.text: You're building a multi-step document processing agent that orchestrates three Skills: document classification, data extraction, and compliance checking. Early in development, you activate all three Skills simultaneously in the system prompt so Claude can access them from the first turn. However, after deploying to production, you notice that token usage per request has grown 35% higher than projected, and Claude sometimes applies the extraction Skill to unclassified documents, causing extraction failures. Your team discovers that progressive disclosure—activating Skills only when their prerequisites are met—could reduce unnecessary context overhead. What is the BEST approach to optimize both token efficiency and task reliability?

options.A: Move all Skills into a single compound Skill definition that enforces a sequential pipeline, requiring Claude to complete one step before the next becomes available.
options.B: Reduce the number of active Skills to one per turn, rotating them manually in response to Claude's output, while storing completed task state in memory for context retrieval.
options.C: Keep all Skills active in the system prompt for simplicity, but add detailed guardrails in each Skill's instructions explicitly forbidding its use until prior Skills complete.
options.D: Implement progressive disclosure by activating Skills conditionally based on task state: classification Skill always active, extraction Skill activated only after successful classification, compliance Skill activated only after extraction completes.

questions.explanation: Progressive disclosure directly addresses both problems: it reduces context overhead by loading only necessary Skills at each stage, and it enforces logical task ordering, preventing extraction from running on unclassified documents. This approach aligns with Skill best practices for efficient context management and task reliability without introducing unnecessary orchestration complexity.

optionExplanations.C: Guardrails in Skill instructions can guide behavior but do not prevent activation or reduce token overhead. Claude may still see and reason about extraction instructions even when classification hasn't occurred, consuming tokens without benefit and risking the same sequencing errors.
optionExplanations.D: Progressive disclosure directly addresses both problems: it reduces context overhead by loading only necessary Skills at each stage, and it enforces logical task ordering, preventing extraction from running on unclassified documents. This approach aligns with Skill best practices for efficient context management and task reliability without introducing unnecessary orchestration complexity.
optionExplanations.A: Combining all Skills into a single compound definition increases the complexity of that definition and doesn't reduce context overhead at the token level. It also reduces modularity and reusability, and may hinder Claude's ability to adapt to variation in document types or requirements.
optionExplanations.B: Manually rotating Skills per turn adds application-layer complexity, introduces latency from explicit state management, and still requires storing full task state in memory, which doesn't reduce token usage relative to progressive disclosure and risks losing context on long runs.

questions.title: Structured Extraction with Missing Optional Fields: Null vs. Omission
questions.text: Your contract extraction system must parse vendor agreements and output a JSON object containing company name (required), contact email (required), renewal date (optional), and special terms (optional). Your evaluation dataset includes 200 contracts, of which 85 lack explicit renewal dates and 120 lack special terms. When you test with a JSON schema that includes these optional fields as nullable (allowing null values), extraction accuracy for the required fields drops 6% compared to a schema that omits optional fields entirely when they're absent. However, downstream systems prefer a consistent JSON structure with all fields always present. What is the MOST PRAGMATIC approach to balance accuracy, consistency, and downstream compatibility?

options.A: Use a schema with all fields always present (nullable optional fields), accepting the 6% accuracy loss as a trade-off for downstream system consistency.
options.B: Add few-shot examples showing contracts with both present and absent optional fields, training the model to output null values explicitly while preserving required-field accuracy.
options.C: Use a schema that omits optional fields when absent, then post-process all outputs to inject null or default values for missing fields before sending to downstream systems.
options.D: Split into two extraction modes: use field-omission schema for accuracy-critical contracts, and full-field schema for less critical contracts, routing each to the appropriate downstream handler.

questions.explanation: This approach preserves extraction accuracy by using a schema optimized for the model's natural behavior (omitting absent optional fields), then applies a lightweight post-processing step to enforce downstream consistency. The 6% accuracy loss on required fields—the most critical data—is avoided, while downstream systems still receive the uniform structure they expect. Post-processing is deterministic and applies no semantic risk.

optionExplanations.A: Accepting a 6% accuracy loss on required fields (company name, email) is a significant cost. These are the highest-value fields; any extraction error there is more damaging than structural inconsistency, and downstream systems can be adapted to handle field omission more easily than upstream systems can recover from missing critical data.
optionExplanations.C: This approach preserves extraction accuracy by using a schema optimized for the model's natural behavior (omitting absent optional fields), then applies a lightweight post-processing step to enforce downstream consistency. The 6% accuracy loss on required fields—the most critical data—is avoided, while downstream systems still receive the uniform structure they expect. Post-processing is deterministic and applies no semantic risk.
optionExplanations.D: Routing contracts to different extraction modes and handlers adds operational complexity and increases the risk of misconfiguration. It also doesn't address the root cause—the schema design—and creates two maintenance burden streams instead of one unified pipeline.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples may nudge the model toward null values, but they don't eliminate the underlying reason for the 6% accuracy drop: nullable fields in the schema introduce ambiguity in how the model reasons about what to extract. The examples don't resolve this; accuracy is likely to remain degraded.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration: Coordinator Bottleneck at Scale
questions.text: You're building a research agent that coordinates three specialized sub-agents (literature_searcher, data_analyst, synthesis_writer) across 50+ simultaneous user queries. Each sub-agent processes independently and returns structured results. Monitoring shows the coordinator agent is becoming a bottleneck—it's sequentially waiting for each sub-agent to complete before sending results to the next, causing 40% latency inflation. The coordinator currently has access to all three tools and manually chains them in sequence within a single conversation. What architectural change best addresses this bottleneck?

options.A: Implement tool caching on the coordinator's system prompt so repeated research patterns are cached, reducing re-planning overhead.
options.B: Add the effort parameter set to 'low' on the coordinator's requests to reduce reasoning overhead and speed up tool orchestration.
options.C: Split the coordinator into three independent stateless agents, each handling one sub-agent, and use an external queue to coordinate handoffs between them.
options.D: Enable parallel tool use so the coordinator calls all three sub-agent tools simultaneously in a single API request, then waits for all results before proceeding.

questions.explanation: Parallel tool use eliminates sequential waiting by allowing the coordinator to invoke all three sub-agents in one API call and collect their results together. This directly removes the 40% latency inflation caused by sequential tool invocation. Tool caching helps with repeated patterns but doesn't solve sequential bottlenecks. Splitting agents adds complexity without fixing the underlying serial dependency. Low effort reduces reasoning but doesn't address the architectural waiting pattern.

optionExplanations.A: Tool caching optimizes repeated invocations of the same tool with similar parameters, but doesn't solve the fundamental problem of waiting for sub-agent 1 to finish before invoking sub-agent 2. The bottleneck is structural (sequential ordering), not computational (reasoning overhead).
optionExplanations.C: Splitting the coordinator into three independent agents introduces state management complexity and inter-agent communication overhead through an external queue. This actually adds latency through coordination infrastructure and doesn't leverage Claude's native parallel execution capability.
optionExplanations.B: The effort parameter trades reasoning depth for speed, but the latency problem stems from waiting for tool results, not from the coordinator's thinking time. Reducing reasoning depth doesn't eliminate the sequential dependency chain.
optionExplanations.D: Parallel tool use allows the coordinator to invoke literature_searcher, data_analyst, and synthesis_writer in a single API request. Claude executes all three tools concurrently at the API level, then returns all results together, eliminating the 40% sequential wait overhead.

questions.title: MCP Server Tool Explosion: 1,200 Tools Causing Callback Failures
questions.text: Your Model Context Protocol server exposes 1,200 domain-specific tools for a financial analysis platform (pricing_check, risk_assessment, compliance_report, audit_trail, etc.). Production logs show that 34% of Claude's tool invocations are failing with 'tool not found' errors, and analysis reveals Claude is inventing tool names that don't exist (e.g., calling get_price_history when only pricing_check is available). Token usage on the discovery request is also exceeding budget. What design pattern best resolves this?

options.A: Use tool search with regex-based filtering so Claude receives only tools matching the current task context, reducing the available tool set from 1,200 to 15–30 per request.
options.B: Implement server-side tool validation that returns helpful error messages with 'did you mean' suggestions, allowing Claude to retry with the correct tool name.
options.C: Split the monolithic MCP server into 12 smaller domain-specific servers (pricing_server, risk_server, compliance_server, etc.) so Claude receives only 100 tools per connection.
options.D: Add an alias mapping layer that accepts both the actual tool name and common synonyms, so 'get_price_history' automatically routes to 'pricing_check'.

questions.explanation: Tool search with regex filtering reduces cognitive load on Claude by presenting only relevant tools for the current task. Instead of Claude choosing from 1,200 tools and guessing names, it sees 15–30 contextually appropriate tools with correct, discoverable names. This eliminates both the 'tool not found' failures (Claude no longer invents names) and token budget overflow (smaller discovery set). Server-side validation and aliases treat the symptom, not the root cause. Multiple servers add operational complexity without solving Claude's visibility problem.

optionExplanations.B: Error handling and retry logic catch failures after Claude has already made a mistake, but don't prevent the core problem: Claude is overwhelmed by 1,200 tool names and defaults to inventing plausible-sounding alternatives. Retries add latency and token cost without solving the root visibility issue.
optionExplanations.A: Tool search narrows the tool set Claude sees based on the current task, reducing 1,200 tools to only the 15–30 relevant ones. This prevents 'tool not found' errors because Claude only sees tools that actually exist and are appropriate, eliminating name invention and token budget bloat.
optionExplanations.C: Splitting into 12 servers shifts the problem to infrastructure management (maintaining multiple MCP server connections, routing logic, connection limits) without solving Claude's core challenge: selecting the correct tool name from a large set. Each domain server still presents 100 tools, which is still overwhelming.
optionExplanations.D: Aliases create alternative names for tools but don't reduce the cognitive load of tool discovery. Claude still has to choose from 1,200 tools; it now just has multiple names for each one, which actually increases confusion and token cost during tool discovery.

questions.title: Structured Extraction: Conflicting Schema Requirements in Production
questions.text: Your customer support extraction pipeline uses strict tool use to extract refund_reason (enum: 'defective', 'unwanted', 'wrong_size', 'other') from support tickets. A recent business rule change requires capturing finer-grained reasons: 'defective' must now be split into 'defective_minor' (cosmetic damage) and 'defective_critical' (non-functional). You cannot modify the downstream database schema for 6 weeks. Your team proposes three solutions: (A) add the new enum values and update extraction logic later, (B) keep the old schema and post-process Claude's output with a separate LLM call, (C) expand the 'other' category to capture detailed reasons as free text within the enum constraint. What minimizes extraction errors and latency during the transition?

options.A: Expand the enum to include 'defective_minor' and 'defective_critical', then implement a compatibility layer that maps them back to 'defective' before database insertion.
options.B: Keep the enum unchanged, but add a secondary 'defect_severity' optional field (enum: 'minor', 'critical') to capture the split without changing the primary schema.
options.C: Retain the original enum and pass Claude's extraction result to a separate LLM call that classifies 'defective' into minor vs. critical, then re-run strict tool use.
options.D: Expand the enum to 'defective_minor' and 'defective_critical', but accept that the database will reject unknown values; implement server-side error handling to gracefully degrade to 'defective'.

questions.explanation: Expanding the enum with new values lets Claude extract accurately into the new schema, then a lightweight compatibility layer (a simple string mapping function) translates 'defective_minor' → 'defective' before database insertion. This approach avoids a second LLM call (latency overhead), doesn't introduce optional fields that could be missed, and doesn't rely on error handling (which implies failures in production). The mapping layer is trivial and temporary, bridging until the database schema updates.

optionExplanations.A: Expanding the enum allows Claude to extract the fine-grained distinctions directly, capturing business intent accurately. A simple compatibility layer (a lookup table or string replace function) maps the new values back to the old schema without requiring a second LLM call or database changes. This is maintainable and low-latency.
optionExplanations.C: A second LLM call with strict tool use doubles latency and token cost for every extraction. It also introduces a secondary failure point: if Claude's first extraction is ambiguous, the second call must re-infer context from partial information, increasing error rate.
optionExplanations.B: Optional fields in schemas can be silently omitted by Claude if not explicitly required, leading to incomplete severity classification. The primary enum still has 'defective' without detail, so the business requirement for fine-grained reasons isn't actually met.
optionExplanations.D: Intentionally allowing database rejection errors is a fragile pattern that relies on error handling logic to recover. It increases debugging complexity and produces degraded results (collapsing to 'defective') when stricter capture was the whole goal. This creates operational brittleness.

questions.title: Context Window Pressure: Long-Running CI/CD Agent Token Budget
questions.text: Your CI/CD agent monitors build logs, test results, and deployment states across 30 repositories. It runs for 8+ hours continuously, maintaining a conversation that includes system prompts (8K tokens), tool definitions (6K tokens), and accumulated turn history (growing at ~2K tokens per 10 minutes). Your 200K context window is reaching 85% capacity after 4 hours of operation. You notice that tool results from completed builds (executed 2+ hours ago) are still in the conversation—they're accurate but no longer relevant to new builds. What strategy best extends the agent's operational window while preserving current task performance?

options.A: Implement compaction to automatically summarize older turns into a condensed narrative when context approaches the window limit, preserving both old build state and new task continuity.
options.B: Enable context editing to automatically clear tool results after 30 minutes, and monitor the conversation to ensure critical build states aren't accidentally discarded.
options.C: Upgrade to a larger context model if available, and implement a conversation save/load mechanism that snapshots state every 2 hours to allow restart if the window fills.
options.D: Enable automatic prompt caching on the system prompt and tool definitions, then manually delete old tool results from the conversation before they consume too much context.

questions.explanation: Compaction automatically summarizes earlier turns (old build results, completed deployments) into a compact narrative summary while keeping recent context (current builds, active tasks) in full detail. This preserves the agent's ability to reference old builds if needed (e.g., 'did build X succeed?') while reclaiming tokens, allowing the agent to run the full 8-hour window. Prompt caching helps but still requires manual cleanup. Context editing risks losing critical state. Model upgrades are costly and don't solve the fundamental accumulation problem.

optionExplanations.D: Prompt caching reduces the cost of reusing the system prompt and tools across turns, but the primary token drain is the 2K-per-10-minutes turn history, which isn't cached. Manual deletion of tool results is error-prone and requires active monitoring; you risk losing a result the agent needs for correlation (e.g., 'this test failed the same way yesterday').
optionExplanations.B: Context editing with a 30-minute TTL is aggressive and risks deleting build results that the agent may need to reference. For example, if a deployment status from 40 minutes ago is required to diagnose a current failure, it's gone. This prioritizes token conservation over task reliability.
optionExplanations.C: Upgrading the model increases cost and doesn't solve the token growth problem—the agent will simply fill a larger window with the same accumulation rate. Save/load snapshots introduce complexity and force restarts, losing continuity that multi-hour monitoring requires.
optionExplanations.A: Compaction summarizes older turns (e.g., 'Between 2:00–4:00, 12 builds were executed: 10 passed, 2 failed in the DNS-resolution layer') into a dense summary, freeing tokens from detailed logs while retaining key facts. The agent can still reason about historical patterns without storing raw results, extending the 8-hour window naturally.

questions.title: Claude Code Skill Distribution: Inconsistent Behavior Across Teams
questions.text: Your organization has built a reusable Skill for 'contract analysis' as markdown instructions in Claude Code, covering clause extraction, risk flagging, and summary generation. Teams A and B both use the Skill, but Team A reports 92% accuracy on risk detection while Team B reports 67% accuracy on the same contract types. Investigation reveals that Team A manually edited the Skill to add 6 custom prompt examples specific to their contract templates, while Team B uses the unmodified version. You need to standardize behavior across teams while allowing future customization. What's the best approach?

options.A: Leave the Skill unmodified, and provide teams with a separate 'Best Practices Guide' documenting Team A's examples so they can manually add examples if desired.
options.B: Merge Team A's custom examples into the canonical Skill, then version it as v2.0 for all teams to adopt, deprecating the old version.
options.C: Create a Skill configuration system where teams can register domain-specific example sets (insurance_examples, SaaS_examples) that Claude Code dynamically loads based on contract classification.
options.D: Extract the contract analysis into a standalone MCP server with pluggable example modules, requiring teams to re-architect their Claude Code integration.

questions.explanation: A Skill configuration system lets the canonical Skill adapt to different contexts without maintaining multiple versions or losing the centralized source of truth. Teams can register domain-specific examples that Claude Code dynamically discovers and applies based on contract type, achieving Team A's 92% accuracy consistently. This is more scalable than manually merging examples (which works until conflicts arise) and more practical than requiring teams to manage custom forks. It avoids unnecessary architectural rework.

optionExplanations.B: Merging Team A's examples into a single v2.0 works initially, but creates a new problem: those examples may be specialized to Team A's contract templates and could degrade accuracy for other teams. Future customization requests from Team C or D force either another merge (creating bloat) or maintenance of multiple versions anyway.
optionExplanations.C: A configuration system allows the Skill to remain canonical while automatically adapting to each team's domain. Teams register their examples (insurance_examples, SaaS_examples) in a configuration layer, and Claude Code loads the appropriate set based on contract classification or team context. This preserves centralized maintenance while enabling domain-specific customization.
optionExplanations.A: Documentation and manual implementation shift the burden to each team. Teams B and C will likely not follow the guide consistently, leading to a patchwork of behaviors and duplication of effort. This doesn't achieve standardization and scales poorly.
optionExplanations.D: Extracting into an MCP server adds architectural complexity and operational overhead (managing a separate server, versioning, deployment) without solving the core problem. Skill systems in Claude Code are designed precisely for this use case; an MCP server is over-engineered.

questions.title: Agent Handoff Timing: When to Stop Investigating and Escalate
questions.text: Your support agent has been investigating a subscription cancellation request for 18 turns. The customer's stated reason is 'too expensive,' but deep investigation reveals three separate billing issues: a promotional discount that was never applied, a duplicate monthly charge from 2 months ago, and an incorrect tier upgrade. Each issue is independently resolvable by the agent within its authorization limits. However, the customer has now written: 'I just want to cancel. Stop digging and let me go.' The agent can escalate to a human specialist who can offer a retention package, or resolve all three billing issues and then ask if cancellation is still desired. What should the agent do?

options.A: Ask the customer which of the three issues is most important to them, then fix only that one issue before offering escalation.
options.B: Immediately escalate to the human specialist without fixing the billing issues, since the customer explicitly requested to stop the investigation.
options.C: Acknowledge the customer's frustration, quickly summarize the three fixable issues, offer to resolve them in the next message, and let the customer decide whether to proceed or escalate.
options.D: Resolve all three billing issues first, then inform the customer they're fixed and ask if they still want to cancel before escalating.

questions.explanation: The customer's explicit request to stop investigating is valid and must be honored, but the agent should respect their agency by transparently presenting the high-value resolution option available to them right now—a quick summary plus the choice. This balances respecting their stated preference (stop investigating) with enabling an informed decision about whether to accept the fix. Escalating without presenting the option wastes both the investigation work and the opportunity to retain them; unilaterally fixing all three issues ignores their stated desire to end the interaction; and asking them to triage further extends the conversation they're already frustrated with.

optionExplanations.B: Immediately escalating respects the customer's request to stop investigating but discards 18 turns of investigation work and misses the opportunity to resolve three independent issues that may be the actual source of dissatisfaction beneath the stated reason. The specialist won't be able to address these technical billing fixes more efficiently.
optionExplanations.D: Silently resolving all three issues without asking first violates the customer's explicit request to stop investigating, even though the outcome might be better. The customer has already said 'stop'—proceeding anyway treats their stated preference as unimportant.
optionExplanations.C: Acknowledging frustration, showing the investigation has found actionable fixes, and offering the choice respects the customer's explicit request while giving them the information needed to make an informed decision. This is maximally respectful of their agency while leveraging the investigation already completed.
optionExplanations.A: Asking the customer to prioritize further extends the investigation they've already requested to end, creating the exact friction they're trying to escape. It also assumes the customer wants to choose which issues to fix rather than accepting a comprehensive resolution.

questions.title: MCP Tool Schema Design: Preventing Category Mismatches at Scale
questions.text: Your finance application exposes an MCP server with a single `record_transaction` tool accepting `category` (string), `amount` (number), and `description` (string). In production over three months, you observe that 11% of agent calls use incompatible category-amount combinations: negative amounts for income categories, zero amounts for expense categories, or extremely large amounts (>$1M) for personal expense categories that should be limited to $10k. You have the option to redesign the tool interface before scaling to 50+ agents across your company. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Keep the single tool but add a `validate_transaction` helper tool the agent must call first to check the transaction before recording, making validation explicit in the workflow.
options.B: Create separate tools: `record_income` (amount > 0, limited categories), `record_expense` (amount > 0, category + amount-limit pairs), and `record_transfer` (any amount, transfer-specific fields) with schema enforcement.
options.C: Add server-side validation that rejects invalid combinations and returns structured error messages, allowing the agent to learn and retry with the correct combination.
options.D: Add detailed examples and constraints in the tool description documenting valid combinations (e.g., 'income categories require positive amounts; expense categories cap at $10k unless flagged as exception') plus enum constraints on category names.

questions.explanation: Splitting into category-specific tools makes invalid combinations structurally impossible—the schema itself enforces the constraints. An expense tool enforces amount limits; an income tool enforces positive amounts. The agent cannot misuse the tool because the wrong parameters simply don't exist for that tool. This eliminates 11% of errors proactively, requires no agent learning, and scales cleanly.

optionExplanations.C: Server-side validation catches errors only after the agent has already made a mistake, forcing retry loops and adding latency. Over 50+ agents, each generating ~11% errors, this creates recurring failure overhead and wastes tokens on error recovery rather than preventing the errors in the first place.
optionExplanations.B: Splitting tools by transaction type makes invalid combinations structurally impossible. The schema enforces category-appropriate amounts, sign constraints, and limits automatically—no documentation, examples, or validation logic needed. The agent cannot misuse the tool.
optionExplanations.D: Documentation and examples guide the agent but do not prevent errors. Agents frequently ignore or misunderstand examples, especially under time pressure or when context is deep. An 11% error rate over three months suggests that guidance-only approaches have already failed to work reliably.
optionExplanations.A: A separate validation tool adds an extra step the agent must execute before every transaction, increasing latency and token consumption. It also leaves the original tool's dangerous combinations still accessible—the agent can skip validation or the validation tool can malfunction, and errors still occur.

questions.title: Claude Code Skill Scope: When to Split Skills vs. Extend Behavior
questions.text: Your Claude Code deployment has a Skill called `analyze_data` that currently handles CSV parsing, basic statistics (mean, median, max), and data visualization (bar charts, histograms). You're seeing request abandonment increase by 8% when users ask for advanced analysis (regression modeling, time-series forecasting) because the Skill doesn't cover these use cases—Claude Code tries to implement them from scratch each time, leading to slower execution and inconsistent code quality. You're considering two approaches: (1) expand the `analyze_data` Skill to include regression and forecasting templates and logic, or (2) create a new separate `advanced_analysis` Skill focused only on regression and forecasting. Which approach minimizes abandonment while maintaining system health?

options.A: Expand the existing Skill to include regression and forecasting; a single unified Skill is easier for users to discover and reduces cognitive load.
options.B: Create a new separate Skill; it keeps concerns isolated and allows Claude Code to selectively apply the advanced Skill only when the user's intent is detected, preserving efficiency for users who don't need it.
options.C: Expand the existing Skill and add a "lite mode" flag that users can toggle to choose between basic and advanced analysis, letting them control scope.
options.D: Create the new Skill but also add it to the existing `analyze_data` Skill's instructions so Claude Code applies both; this ensures coverage without splitting functionality.

questions.explanation: Separate Skills allow selective application—Claude Code applies the advanced_analysis Skill only when the user's intent signals a need for regression or forecasting. This means basic users get faster responses with the lightweight analyze_data Skill, while advanced users get the appropriate advanced Skill. Progressive disclosure is built into the Skill mechanism. Expanding the single Skill bloats context even for users who won't use the advanced features, slowing all executions and violating the principle of loading only what's needed.

optionExplanations.A: Expanding a single Skill increases its size and context overhead. Even users asking for simple statistics now have the full regression/forecasting code loaded into Claude Code's context, slowing their requests. The feature consolidation advantage does not outweigh the performance cost, especially since Skill discovery is designed to support multiple related Skills.
optionExplanations.B: Separate Skills enable progressive disclosure. Claude Code applies analyze_data for basic requests and advanced_analysis only when needed, keeping context efficient for typical use cases while enabling advanced functionality on demand. This directly addresses the abandonment issue—advanced users get what they need without waiting for bloated context, and basic users don't pay the cost.
optionExplanations.D: Adding the advanced Skill to the existing Skill's instructions creates the same bloat problem as expansion; Claude Code still loads both sets of instructions for every request. It also muddles the intent detection—Claude Code has to reason about which Skill to use even though both are always loaded.
optionExplanations.C: A user-controlled flag requires the user to understand the performance implications and remember to toggle it; this adds friction and shifts cognitive load to the user. It also doesn't solve the default case—most users won't set the flag optimally, so abandonment persists for those who don't know about or use the flag.

questions.title: Structured Output Design: Balancing Completeness vs. Agent Reliability
questions.text: Your customer support agent uses a structured output schema to extract refund decisions with fields: `refund_eligible` (boolean), `reason` (string), `supporting_evidence` (array of strings), and `confidence_score` (0-1 float). In production, the agent generates refund decisions 94% of the time without errors, but the remaining 6% show two failure modes: (1) when evidence is sparse, the agent skips `supporting_evidence` entirely rather than returning an empty array, breaking downstream processing (2%) and (2) when the decision is nuanced (e.g., 'partial refund'), the agent returns `refund_eligible: true` with a reason like 'only 50% eligible,' confusing policy enforcement systems (4%). You're considering schema redesigns. Which change best reduces both failure modes?

options.A: Simplify the schema by removing the boolean `refund_eligible` field entirely, replacing it with a required `refund_decision` enum (APPROVE, APPROVE_PARTIAL, DENY) and required `refund_percentage` field (0-100).
options.B: Change `refund_eligible` to an enum: FULL, PARTIAL, NONE, and add a required `refund_percentage` field; restructure `supporting_evidence` as a required array with a minimum of 0 items.
options.C: Add validation logic server-side that detects missing `supporting_evidence` and substitutes an empty array, and validates that `refund_eligible: true` matches a refund_percentage > 0.
options.D: Add `refund_percentage` (0-100) as a required field alongside `refund_eligible`, forcing the agent to express nuance explicitly and making the schema cover partial refunds.

questions.explanation: Replacing the ambiguous boolean with a multi-state enum (`APPROVE_PARTIAL` exists as its own decision type) eliminates the contradiction between `refund_eligible: true` and 'partial refund' text. The mandatory `refund_percentage` field forces explicit quantification. Removing the boolean prevents the agent from defaulting to true/false when the real answer is 'partial.' This directly addresses both failure modes: nuance is structurally unavoidable, and a required percentage field ensures completeness.

optionExplanations.D: Adding `refund_percentage` without restructuring `refund_eligible` doesn't eliminate the core problem: the agent can still return `refund_eligible: true` with `refund_percentage: 50`, which is logically contradictory and still confuses policy systems. The boolean remains a footgun.
optionExplanations.B: An enum for the decision type is good, but keeping the separate `refund_eligible` boolean creates redundancy and potential contradiction—the agent still has to maintain two representations of the same decision. The `supporting_evidence` fix is correct, but the dual-representation design problem remains.
optionExplanations.C: Server-side validation is a patch that catches errors after the fact rather than making them impossible. The agent can still generate the contradiction; the server just cleans it up. This adds latency and doesn't prevent the confusion from happening—it only detects it after the fact.
optionExplanations.A: A single required enum (`APPROVE`, `APPROVE_PARTIAL`, `DENY`) with a mandatory percentage field eliminates redundancy and makes partial decisions a first-class decision type, not an anomaly. The agent cannot express nuance using the wrong field because the field doesn't exist; it must use the correct enum value. This directly solves both failure modes proactively.

questions.title: Context Window Budget: Trading Analysis Depth for Conversation Length in a Research Agent
questions.text: Your research agent conducts multi-turn investigations (average 40 turns) for academic fact-checking. Currently, it uses a 200k token context window. Early turns include deep document analysis (5–10k tokens per turn for tool results like web fetch and PDF parsing). By turn 30, the agent has exhausted ~120k tokens on analysis results and context, leaving only 80k for the final 10 turns. At this point, the agent's responses become superficial—it skips cross-referencing conflicting sources, avoids deep reasoning, and produces lower-quality conclusions. You need to maintain long conversations while preserving analysis quality. Which strategy best solves this without increasing model costs significantly?

options.A: Limit conversations to 25 turns instead of 40, forcing earlier conclusions; this guarantees sufficient budget per turn but sacrifices thoroughness.
options.B: Progressively summarize early-turn analysis results using compaction when context approaches 150k tokens, condensing detailed findings into structured summaries to free space for ongoing investigation.
options.C: Split the research task into two separate agents: one handles turns 1–20 (early analysis), summarizes findings, then passes a handoff document to a second agent for turns 21–40 (final synthesis).
options.D: Implement prompt caching (5-minute window) on early-turn document results so they aren't fully re-transmitted; this reduces redundant token cost and preserves budget for later analysis.

questions.explanation: Compaction automatically summarizes earlier conversation context when approaching the window limit, condensing verbose tool results (fetches, PDFs) into crisp summaries while preserving the key findings. This frees ~40–50k tokens for the final 10 turns without truncating the conversation or changing task structure. The agent retains full reasoning capability in the final turns because the high-value findings are retained in condensed form. Caching helps but doesn't directly solve the core problem of accumulating result tokens; splitting agents adds complexity and handoff overhead.

optionExplanations.D: Prompt caching reduces re-transmission costs but doesn't address the core problem: early-turn results are transmitted once and fully cached, but they still consume tokens in context. The 120k problem persists. Caching helps at the API level but doesn't free context space for later turns.
optionExplanations.B: Compaction automatically condenses early analysis results into summaries at a configurable threshold (e.g., 150k tokens). Detailed fetches and PDFs become crisp findings; the agent retains the key information it needs for cross-referencing and reasoning in later turns, while freeing 40–50k tokens. This directly solves the depth problem by preserving budget where it matters most—final synthesis.
optionExplanations.A: Truncating conversations avoids the problem rather than solving it. Limiting to 25 turns means the agent never reaches deep synthesis on complex topics. This is a regression in capability, not a solution.
optionExplanations.C: Splitting into two agents adds handoff complexity, requires designing summaries to pass between agents, and risks losing context that the second agent needs for synthesis. It also introduces latency and failure modes (handoff quality, agent 2 misunderstanding agent 1's findings). It's over-engineered compared to compaction.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Workflow – Escalation Without Pre-Analysis
questions.text: You are building a multi-agent research system where a coordinator agent routes queries to specialized agents (market research, technical analysis, competitive intelligence). A user submits a complex request about a competitor's AI capabilities, but the coordinator immediately hands off to the competitive intelligence agent without first calling a query_parsing tool to decompose the request. The downstream agent now struggles to identify which sub-questions to tackle first, resulting in redundant API calls and delayed response. What should the coordinator agent's workflow prioritize?

options.A: Always parse and decompose the user query first into atomic sub-questions before routing to specialized agents.
options.B: Use tool parallel execution to send the raw query to all three specialized agents simultaneously and merge their outputs.
options.C: Implement a feedback loop where the specialized agent returns clarification requests to the coordinator if the query is ambiguous.
options.D: Route requests directly to specialized agents to minimize latency; redundant calls are acceptable if the final answer is accurate.

questions.explanation: Query decomposition before routing reduces downstream confusion, eliminates redundant tool calls, and lets the coordinator direct each agent to its specific responsibility. This is a best practice in orchestration to avoid wasted context and API overhead.

optionExplanations.D: Latency gains are negated by the cost of redundant API calls and repeated analysis across agents.
optionExplanations.A: Pre-parsing clarifies intent, distributes work efficiently, and minimizes backtracking between agents.
optionExplanations.C: Clarification loops add round-trip latency and degrade user experience when the coordinator could have parsed upfront.
optionExplanations.B: Parallel execution to all agents wastes computational resources on unfiltered, overlapping analyses rather than targeted delegation.

questions.title: MCP Server Tool Definitions – Naming Collisions Across Namespaces
questions.text: Your Model Context Protocol server exposes tools from two data sources: a legacy CRM system and a modern customer data platform (CDP). Both have a 'get_customer' tool that accept customer IDs but return different field schemas (CRM returns legacy_id, email, phone; CDP returns unified_id, email, phone, lifecycle_stage, ltv_score). When Claude receives both tool definitions, it cannot reliably predict which one to call without explicit guidance. How should you structure the MCP server to prevent this collision?

options.A: Rename one tool to 'get_customer_legacy' and the other to 'get_customer_unified' with clear descriptions of what each returns.
options.B: Create a single merged tool that queries both sources and returns a combined result to avoid ambiguity.
options.C: Implement a router tool that accepts a 'source' parameter and internally dispatches to the correct backend.
options.D: Use tool grouping metadata to tag each tool with its source system and let Claude select based on context.

questions.explanation: Explicit, distinct tool names in the schema with clear descriptions eliminate ambiguity and let Claude reliably select the correct tool based on its knowledge of which fields the user needs or which system context is relevant.

optionExplanations.A: Distinct names and descriptions in the tool schema make intent unambiguous and align with MCP best practices for namespacing.
optionExplanations.C: A single router tool obscures the semantic difference between sources and forces Claude to make an extra hop; less transparent.
optionExplanations.B: Merging results adds latency and may return irrelevant fields; splitting them allows targeted queries.
optionExplanations.D: Metadata grouping is not a standard MCP mechanism; tool names and descriptions are the primary discovery layer.

questions.title: Structured Extraction – Avoiding Hallucination in Optional Fields
questions.text: You are extracting product review data into a strict JSON schema with fields: rating (1–5), reviewer_name, review_text, verified_purchase (boolean), and discount_code_mentioned (string, optional). During testing, you find that 15% of reviews without a discount code mentioned still generate plausible-sounding fictional codes (e.g., 'SUMMER20', 'SAVE10'). The schema defines discount_code_mentioned as nullable, but Claude still hallucinates. What is the most direct fix?

options.A: Add explicit instruction to the system prompt: 'If discount code is not mentioned in the review text, return null for discount_code_mentioned. Do not generate or infer codes.'
options.B: Switch to a boolean field 'has_discount_code' (true/false) instead of allowing string values.
options.C: Remove the discount_code_mentioned field from the schema entirely to prevent hallucination.
options.D: Post-process all extracted records with a secondary LLM call to verify each discount code against a known list of valid promotional codes.

questions.explanation: Explicit permission and instruction to return null for missing information directly counteracts the model's tendency to hallucinate plausible values. This is the root-cause fix without removing useful optional fields or adding expensive verification layers.

optionExplanations.C: Removing the field discards valuable data when discount codes are legitimately mentioned in some reviews.
optionExplanations.A: Clear instruction to return null for unstated information prevents hallucination while preserving the field for valid extractions.
optionExplanations.B: A boolean is lower fidelity; you lose the actual code value when it is mentioned, reducing extraction utility.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing verification adds latency and cost; the model should follow instructions upfront rather than require cleanup.

questions.title: Context Window Management – Balancing Conversation History vs. System Constraints
questions.text: Your customer support agent has been chatting with a customer for 45 exchanges over 2 hours, accumulating ~180k tokens of conversation history. The customer asks a new question about their billing cycle. The agent is approaching the 200k token limit of Claude 3.5 Sonnet. You have three options: include full history for context richness, use compaction to summarize early exchanges, or switch to a larger model mid-conversation. Which approach best balances cost, latency, and context quality?

options.A: Use server-side compaction to summarize the first 30 exchanges into a concise summary, keeping recent exchanges verbatim.
options.B: Switch immediately to Claude Opus (200k window) to avoid truncation and preserve all conversation history.
options.C: Send the full 180k tokens and accept a longer response latency due to processing load.
options.D: Truncate the history to the last 10 exchanges and rely on the agent's explicit in-conversation memory of prior issues.

questions.explanation: Compaction preserves contextual richness from early interactions (e.g., account setup issues, prior complaints) while keeping recent conversation verbatim for immediate relevance. It avoids model switching cost, stays within the current token budget, and maintains a searchable audit trail.

optionExplanations.B: Model switching introduces billing complexity and latency overhead; compaction achieves the same goal more efficiently within the current model.
optionExplanations.A: Compaction summarizes early context efficiently, keeps recent exchanges for immediate relevance, and maintains cost efficiency.
optionExplanations.D: Truncating loses valuable historical context (prior issues, account history) that may be essential for resolving the current question.
optionExplanations.C: Sending full tokens near the limit increases latency and risks token overage; compaction solves this without truncation penalties.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Handoff with Incomplete Context
questions.text: You're building a multi-agent research system where Agent A (researcher) gathers data and passes it to Agent B (synthesizer) for report generation. Agent A frequently completes its search after finding 60–70% of relevant sources, then hands off incomplete findings to Agent B. Agent B then hallucinates missing citations to fill gaps. What is the most effective way to prevent this failure mode?

options.A: Increase Agent A's token budget so it searches longer and more exhaustively before any handoff occurs.
options.B: Add a validation agent (Agent C) that fact-checks Agent B's output against the original sources provided by Agent A.
options.C: Switch to a single-agent design where one model handles both research and synthesis sequentially to eliminate inter-agent communication overhead.
options.D: Modify Agent A's termination logic to require explicit confirmation from Agent B before handoff, ensuring B has seen all gathered data and can request additional searches.

questions.explanation: The root cause is premature handoff without Agent B's input. Requiring B to review findings and request additional searches before handoff addresses the coordination failure directly, preventing hallucination downstream while keeping the multi-agent architecture intact.

optionExplanations.B: Post-hoc fact-checking catches hallucinations but doesn't prevent them; Agent B still generates false citations first, wasting tokens and latency.
optionExplanations.D: Explicit handoff confirmation ensures Agent B has all necessary context and can request additional research, eliminating the information gap that triggers hallucination.
optionExplanations.A: Longer searches may find more sources, but without B's feedback on what's missing, A cannot know what to prioritize; this is a throughput fix, not a coordination fix.
optionExplanations.C: Single-agent designs lose the parallelism and specialization benefits of multi-agent systems and don't address the root coordination issue.

questions.title: MCP Server Tool Proliferation and Context Window Bloat
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes 240 tools to Claude (database queries, file operations, API integrations, logging). During testing, you observe that Claude frequently calls the wrong tool (e.g., querying the file system when it should query the database) and response latency is 3–4 seconds even on simple requests. Analysis shows 65% of tokens are consumed by tool definitions alone. What is the best approach to improve accuracy and latency?

options.A: Batch related tools into composite mega-tools that perform multiple operations in a single call.
options.B: Reduce the 240 tools to a core 30-tool set and move the rest into a separate optional MCP server the user must explicitly enable.
options.C: Implement tool search with regex-based filtering so Claude only sees tools matching the current task category.
options.D: Add detailed natural-language descriptions to every tool so Claude has better context for tool selection.

questions.explanation: Tool search with regex filtering dynamically loads only relevant tools based on context, drastically reducing token overhead while improving selection accuracy by eliminating irrelevant options from consideration. This scales to hundreds of tools without manual tool set curation.

optionExplanations.C: Tool search eliminates context window bloat by showing only task-relevant tools, improving both accuracy and latency at scale.
optionExplanations.B: Manually splitting tools into separate servers adds user friction and doesn't solve the core problem; it just hides it behind an enable/disable gate.
optionExplanations.D: Better descriptions help marginally, but if Claude sees 240 descriptions, token cost and selection confusion remain high.
optionExplanations.A: Mega-tools reduce token count slightly but increase latency per call (more complex operations) and reduce Claude's ability to pick the right single-purpose tool.

questions.title: Structured Extraction: Distinguishing Null from Absent Field
