The context window is 200k tokens; compaction is enabled but hasn't triggered yet. The agent is about to retrieve and analyze paper #140, but you observe token usage climbing sharply. What should you do?

options.A: Let compaction handle it automatically. The system will trigger compaction before hitting the hard limit, so no manual intervention is needed.
options.B: Switch to batch mode for the remaining papers to avoid real-time token pressure and process them asynchronously in smaller groups.
options.C: Manually delete the summaries for papers #1–50 from the conversation history to create space, prioritizing recent papers for context.
options.D: Proactively call the compaction API to summarize papers #1–100 into a consolidated summary before the next paper retrieval, freeing ~200k tokens.

questions.explanation: Proactive compaction before hitting the window ceiling gives Claude a clean summary of earlier work while preserving recency and coherence for the current analysis. This prevents mid-task failures and maintains session continuity.

optionExplanations.A: Relying entirely on automatic compaction risks triggering too late, causing latency spikes or token errors during critical analysis moments.
optionExplanations.D: Preemptive compaction keeps the session running smoothly by converting dense history into efficient summaries without losing content fidelity.
optionExplanations.B: Switching to batch mode mid-session breaks the continuity of analysis and makes it harder to cross-reference earlier papers with new findings.
optionExplanations.C: Manual deletion is uncontrolled and risks losing important context; compaction is designed to preserve semantic content while reducing tokens.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration: Task Routing Under Uncertainty
questions.text: You're building a multi-agent research system where a coordinator agent must decide whether to route complex financial analysis tasks to a specialized finance agent or allow a general-purpose agent to handle them. The finance agent has high accuracy (94%) but limited availability (serves 3 concurrent users). The general agent has 78% accuracy but unlimited capacity. You observe that 40% of tasks timeout waiting for the finance agent, causing customer escalations. What is the most effective approach to reduce timeouts while maintaining quality?

options.A: Upgrade the finance agent's infrastructure to handle 10 concurrent users, eliminating the bottleneck entirely.
options.B: Implement a queue with priority scoring—route high-value financial tasks to the specialist, route routine tasks to the general agent, and escalate timeouts to humans after 30 seconds.
options.C: Implement dynamic routing based on task complexity scoring and current queue depth—if the finance queue exceeds 2 tasks, auto-route to the general agent with a confidence threshold filter.
options.D: Switch all tasks to the general agent and post-process results with a verification step using the finance agent as a fact-checker only.

questions.explanation: Dynamic routing with queue-aware decision logic balances quality and availability by respecting the specialist's constraints while maintaining SLAs. This avoids permanent quality loss (B), arbitrary timeouts (A), and assumes unlimited infrastructure scaling (C) which may be costly.

optionExplanations.B: A hard timeout may escalate routine issues unnecessarily and doesn't adapt to actual queue conditions; priority scoring alone doesn't solve the capacity problem.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing cannot recover accuracy lost during initial analysis; using the specialist only for verification wastes their strength and still creates bottlenecks.
optionExplanations.A: Scaling infrastructure solves capacity but doesn't address whether every task truly needs the specialist; may be inefficient cost-wise.
optionExplanations.C: This approach respects specialist constraints, adapts to live queue pressure, and applies confidence thresholds to protect quality on lower-complexity tasks routed to the general agent.

questions.title: MCP Tool Contract Definition: Schema Versioning and Breaking Changes
questions.text: Your team maintains an MCP server that exposes a `lookup_customer` tool used by 15 different client applications. You need to add a new required parameter `region_code` to improve accuracy. Adding it as required will break all existing clients unless they update their callers. However, you could make it optional with a default fallback. What is the most robust approach for managing this tool schema evolution?

options.A: Make `region_code` optional with a sensible default, increment the tool version in metadata, and document the breaking change in release notes for future mandatory adoption.
options.B: Make `region_code` required immediately and send a broadcast notification to all known client teams requesting urgent updates.
options.C: Keep the old tool behavior unchanged and create a separate `lookup_customer_with_region` tool alongside the original, allowing clients to opt-in incrementally.
options.D: Create a new tool `lookup_customer_v2` with the required parameter, deprecate the old tool immediately, and set a 30-day deadline for all clients to migrate.

questions.explanation: Optional with a default, version signaling, and clear documentation allows graceful backward compatibility while signaling intent for future mandatory adoption. This minimizes immediate breakage while maintaining a clear migration path for consuming teams.

optionExplanations.A: This approach balances compatibility with future clarity—optional parameter doesn't break clients, versioning signals evolution, and documentation enables planned migration.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a new versioned tool is disruptive and forces migration within an arbitrary timeline; tools with identical purposes confuse clients.
optionExplanations.B: Immediate breaking changes cause production incidents across dependent systems; urgent migrations introduce error risk.
optionExplanations.C: Proliferating tool variants (lookup_customer vs. lookup_customer_with_region) bloats the tool namespace and confuses clients about which tool to use; doesn't solve the core versioning problem.

questions.title: Structured Output Validation: Handling Model Confidence Gaps in Extraction
questions.text: Your e-commerce extraction system uses Claude with strict JSON schema to extract product details (name, price, description, sku) from web pages. You notice that for 12% of products, the model fills in plausible but incorrect SKU values when the SKU is not visible in the page HTML. The SKU field is optional in your schema. Adding strict validation rules that require SKU-format compliance would reject valid extractions when the SKU is genuinely absent. What is the best strategy to reduce false SKU hallucination while preserving valid extraction?

options.A: Use the `tool_choice` parameter to require the model to call a `verify_sku_exists` tool before populating the SKU field, blocking hallucinated values.
options.B: Add explicit prompt instructions stating 'If SKU is not visible in the HTML, return null instead of guessing a plausible value.'
options.C: Implement strict regex validation in the schema that only accepts SKUs matching your company's known format; reject the extraction if format doesn't match.
options.D: Use a post-processing LLM call to verify each SKU against the original HTML; if not found, set it to null.

questions.explanation: Direct prompt instruction telling the model to return null for absent fields addresses the root cause—hallucination due to missing guidance—without adding latency or requiring external verification. This is the most efficient and direct intervention.

optionExplanations.D: Post-processing with a second LLM call adds latency and cost; verification logic still relies on the model understanding the HTML, which is the original failure mode.
optionExplanations.B: Explicit instruction to return null for missing information directly corrects the hallucination behavior by giving the model clear permission and expectation.
optionExplanations.C: Format-based validation only catches SKUs that don't match your pattern, but many companies use variable formats; this may reject valid extractions and doesn't prevent hallucination of correctly-formatted fake SKUs.
optionExplanations.A: A verification tool adds complexity and latency; the model's inability to accurately determine presence/absence from HTML is the core issue, not the need for a tool.

questions.title: Context Window Management: Balancing Memory Retention and Token Costs in Long-Running Agent
questions.text: Your customer support agent conducts multi-turn interactions averaging 25 messages over 2 hours. With a 1M-token context window, you observe that: (1) token usage per request grows linearly with conversation history, (2) 18% of conversations approach the 500k-token mark requiring intervention, (3) customer satisfaction drops when context is manually truncated because the agent loses key prior resolution attempts. You have access to automatic prompt caching (5-min duration) and context compaction (Beta). What is the best approach to maintain context quality while controlling costs?

options.A: Implement automatic prompt caching on the system prompt and initial customer summary; this minimizes repeated context encoding without sacrificing conversation history.
options.B: Enable context compaction when context exceeds 400k tokens, allowing the API to automatically summarize earlier turns and preserve the agent's resolution reasoning.
options.C: Manually truncate the oldest 10 messages whenever token count exceeds 300k to keep memory fresh and reduce costs per request.
options.D: Store conversation history in an external database and retrieve only the last 5 messages plus semantically similar prior messages using vector search, reducing raw context size.

questions.explanation: Context compaction automatically summarizes earlier parts of the conversation while preserving semantic meaning and resolution context. It maintains quality and keeps the full context window available for later turns, unlike truncation (C), and addresses the scaling problem better than caching alone (A).

optionExplanations.A: Caching the system prompt and initial summary helps with repeated calls but doesn't address the growth of conversation history within a single long-running session; the per-turn cost still grows.
optionExplanations.B: Automatic compaction preserves resolution reasoning and context quality while keeping the window efficient; Beta status is acceptable for handling 18% of conversations that exceed 500k.
optionExplanations.C: Manual truncation causes the observed customer satisfaction drop because the agent loses prior resolution attempts and context; this is the problem you're trying to solve.
optionExplanations.D: Vector search retrieval adds external infrastructure complexity and latency; the agent loses continuous context of the full conversation thread, potentially confusing multi-step resolutions.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Workflow with Shared Context
questions.text: You're building a multi-agent research system where Agent A (researcher) gathers data, Agent B (analyzer) processes findings, and Agent C (writer) summarizes results. Each agent runs in sequence with the prior agent's output as context. After three cycles, your context window shows 65% utilization with 350K tokens used. Agent A's next data collection could add 80K tokens. What should you do to maintain reliability and cost-efficiency?

options.A: Proceed without changes; 65% utilization leaves ample buffer for the next cycle.
options.B: Implement context compaction after Agent B completes to summarize earlier cycles before Agent A's next run.
options.C: Switch to a smaller model for Agent C to free up tokens before Agent A runs.
options.D: Enable prompt caching on Agent A's system instructions and the prior research cycle outputs to reduce redundant token consumption.

questions.explanation: Prompt caching directly addresses the repeated patterns across cycles—Agent A's instructions and the prior research outputs appear in every iteration. This reduces prompt token cost without losing fidelity or changing agent logic.

optionExplanations.A: While 65% utilization seems safe, adding 80K tokens pushes total toward 430K, risking overflow if subsequent agents add context. No proactive optimization.
optionExplanations.D: Caching leverages the fact that Agent A's instructions and prior cycle outputs are static across iterations, lowering per-request cost and latency while maintaining full context.
optionExplanations.C: Model downgrade is a blunt instrument that affects output quality across all tasks, not just token usage. It doesn't solve the core problem of repeated context.
optionExplanations.B: Compaction trades away detailed prior work by summarizing it, reducing Agent C's ability to reference specific findings and harming research quality.

questions.title: MCP Tool Discovery at Scale with Regex Filtering
questions.text: Your organization exposes 2,400 internal MCP tools spanning HR, finance, compliance, and engineering domains. A customer support agent currently receives all tool definitions upfront, consuming 180K tokens just for the tool manifest. Latency is 3.2 seconds per request. You want to enable the agent to handle 50% more concurrent requests without increasing API costs or response time. Which approach best addresses the constraint?

options.A: Upgrade to a higher-tier model with larger context windows to accommodate all tools without slowdown.
options.B: Use prompt caching to freeze the full tool manifest, reducing per-request overhead by 50%.
options.C: Implement tool search with regex-based filtering so only domain-relevant tools (e.g., HR tools when handling payroll questions) are loaded dynamically.
options.D: Create five separate agent instances, each with a subset of 480 tools, and route requests by department keyword.

questions.explanation: Tool search with regex filtering dynamically loads only domain-relevant tools at inference time, eliminating the 180K token overhead for unrelated tool definitions while preserving the agent's access to the full toolset. This is the intended use case for tool search.

optionExplanations.C: Tool search with regex filtering is purpose-built for this scenario: it reduces manifest context on every request while enabling discovery of domain-specific tools on demand.
optionExplanations.D: Splitting into five instances requires complex routing logic, increases operational overhead, and still carries the same per-instance token cost. Does not scale cost-efficiently.
optionExplanations.B: Caching the 180K manifest reduces cost slightly, but latency gains are minimal because the agent must still parse and reason over all 2,400 definitions each request.
optionExplanations.A: Higher context windows do not reduce per-request token cost or latency; the agent still processes 180K tokens of unrelated tools every inference cycle.

questions.title: Structured Extraction with Ambiguous Real-World Data
questions.text: You're extracting customer payment methods from support tickets into a strict JSON schema with fields: {payment_method: enum["credit_card", "bank_account", "digital_wallet"], confidence: float, notes: string}. The model frequently marks payment_method as "credit_card" with high confidence when the ticket only mentions "we accept cards" without specifying the customer's actual payment method. How should you fix the hallucination?

options.A: Fine-tune the model on 5,000 ticket–extraction pairs to improve instruction-following on this specific domain.
options.B: Switch to an open-text field instead of an enum, allowing the model to output the raw customer statement.
options.C: Revise the prompt to require the model to cite the exact sentence from the ticket that supports each enum choice, and return null if no direct evidence exists.
options.D: Add a confidence threshold filter in post-processing that rejects any extraction with confidence > 0.8 if notes contradict the enum value.

questions.explanation: Requiring citation and explicit evidence grounds the model's classification in the source document. If no direct statement about the customer's chosen method exists, returning null is the correct behavior rather than inferring from company policy.

optionExplanations.D: Confidence thresholds don't prevent hallucination—the model can assign high confidence to incorrect values. The root issue is lack of grounding, not calibration.
optionExplanations.C: Citations force the model to justify enum choices with source text; when no direct evidence exists, null is the appropriate output, preventing plausible but unfounded inferences.
optionExplanations.B: Open text defeats the purpose of structured output for downstream systems that rely on the enum categories. It trades schema conformance for reduced hallucination.
optionExplanations.A: Fine-tuning is expensive and slow; a prompt engineering fix is faster, more auditable, and more cost-effective for this type of grounding problem.

questions.title: Long-Running CI/CD Workflow with Mid-Conversation Rollback
questions.text: Your CI/CD pipeline runs a code review agent across 40 commits in sequence. After reviewing 25 commits and adding detailed feedback to the conversation, the agent is asked to "revert all feedback for the last 10 commits because they're being rebased." The conversation context is now 420K tokens. If you use context_editing to clear the tool results from those 10 commits, what is the most likely outcome?

options.A: Clearing tool results breaks the agent's state machine; it must restart the entire review from commit 1.
options.B: The model's reasoning about earlier commits (1–15) remains intact while the feedback for commits 16–25 is removed, freeing ~40K tokens and allowing the agent to continue.
options.C: Context editing is not applicable mid-conversation; you must close the session and restart with a fresh context window.
options.D: The model hallucinates missing feedback for the removed commits since the system message was not updated to reflect the deletion.

questions.explanation: Context editing removes specific tool results while preserving the conversation history and model state. Clearing the 10 commits' feedback (~40K tokens) allows the agent to continue from commit 26 with earlier context intact, enabling efficient rollback without restart.

optionExplanations.B: Context editing is designed to selectively remove tool results (e.g., from a specific batch of commits) while preserving prior reasoning and reducing token usage for continued inference.
optionExplanations.A: Context editing removes tool results, not the model's reasoning. The agent's state remains coherent; it simply no longer has the results from those 10 commits.
optionExplanations.D: The model does not hallucinate missing tool results; it understands that those results have been removed. The system message context remains valid.
optionExplanations.C: Context editing is a supported mid-conversation feature in the Claude API for managing long-running sessions; it does not require restarting.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration with Conflicting Results
questions.text: You're building a multi-agent research system where three specialized agents (market analyst, competitor researcher, technical expert) independently investigate a product opportunity. The agents return conflicting assessments: analyst says "high market demand," competitor researcher says "saturated market," and technical expert says "feasibility unclear." How should your orchestration layer handle this disagreement before escalating to a human decision-maker?

options.A: Halt the workflow and require the user to manually mediate between agents before proceeding to the next stage.
options.B: Implement a consensus-seeking prompt that asks each agent to review the others' findings and either defend their position with new evidence or revise their assessment.
options.C: Route all three reports directly to the human without modification, preserving agent autonomy and letting the human resolve the conflict.
options.D: Automatically choose the most optimistic assessment (analyst's report) since it indicates the highest potential value.

questions.explanation: A consensus-seeking pattern leverages agent capabilities to resolve disagreements through reasoning rather than either discarding information (B) or creating friction (D). Passing unmediated conflicts directly (A) abdicates the orchestrator's responsibility to synthesize multi-agent outputs into actionable intelligence.

optionExplanations.C: While preserving transparency, unmediated conflict escalation forces humans to do synthesis work the orchestrator could handle, reducing efficiency.
optionExplanations.D: Optimism bias leads to poor decisions; choosing the most favorable report ignores legitimate concerns raised by other agents.
optionExplanations.B: This approach preserves each agent's reasoning while prompting re-evaluation, often revealing shared ground or identifying which agent has incomplete information.
optionExplanations.A: Manual mediation creates bottlenecks and defeats the purpose of multi-agent parallelization; it should be a last resort only.

questions.title: MCP Server Tool Namespace Collision with High-Traffic Integration
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes 150 tools across three resource domains (CRM, billing, support). A high-traffic customer integration discovers that tool names from two domains overlap (e.g., both expose a `create_ticket` tool). Claude sometimes routes requests to the wrong domain, causing billing records to be created as support tickets. What is the most robust fix?

options.A: Add runtime disambiguation logic in the MCP server that inspects the request context and automatically routes to the correct domain handler.
options.B: Split the two colliding domains into separate MCP servers, each with its own connection, to eliminate namespace collision at the protocol level.
options.C: Rename all tools in one domain to include a prefix (e.g., `billing_create_ticket`, `support_create_ticket`) and update client documentation.
options.D: Implement tool use with strict mode to force the model to include an extra parameter specifying the intended domain before executing any tool.

questions.explanation: Renaming tools with domain prefixes is the simplest, most transparent solution that prevents confusion at the point of tool discovery and definition. Tool names are the primary signal Claude uses for selection; explicit naming eliminates ambiguity without adding complexity to orchestration or requiring multiple server connections.

optionExplanations.C: This is the most direct and maintainable solution; it addresses the root cause (ambiguous names) and aligns with tool design best practices of explicit, context-aware naming.
optionExplanations.A: Runtime disambiguation adds latency and complexity, and Claude may still select the wrong tool initially before the server corrects it, leading to wasted tokens and retries.
optionExplanations.D: Strict mode helps with schema conformance but doesn't solve the tool *discovery* problem; Claude must first identify which tool to call, and naming ambiguity happens at that stage.
optionExplanations.B: Splitting into separate servers increases operational overhead and connection management complexity unnecessarily; the issue is naming, not server architecture.

questions.title: Extracting Numeric Metrics with Varied Formats and Units
questions.text: Your extraction pipeline processes financial reports in multiple languages and formats, pulling quarterly revenue figures. Extracted values vary wildly in unit and scale: "€2.5M," "2,500,000 USD," "2.5 billions," "2500000". Your downstream analytics expect a single normalized schema with fields {amount_usd: number, currency: string, scale: string}. What prompt instruction change would most reliably reduce post-processing errors?

options.A: Include a few shot examples in the prompt showing three different input formats ("€2.5M," "2,500,000 USD," "2.5B") with their corresponding normalized outputs in the target schema.
options.B: Add an instruction: 'Always extract numeric values exactly as written in the source, including units and commas, then validate with a second extraction pass.'
options.C: Specify in the schema that all monetary amounts must be converted to USD using current exchange rates, with the currency field indicating the original currency before conversion.
options.D: Request that the model output both the extracted value and the source text verbatim, then add a deterministic post-processing step to parse and normalize.

questions.explanation: Few-shot examples provide Claude with concrete reference patterns for how to interpret varied formats and normalize them consistently. Examples ground the model's behavior more reliably than abstract instructions and reduce hallucination of exchange rates or unit conversions that aren't in the source document.

optionExplanations.B: Preserving the source format defeats the normalization goal and requires more complex downstream parsing, increasing error surface area.
optionExplanations.C: Instructing the model to look up "current exchange rates" introduces external dependencies and hallucination risk; rates change daily and the model may invent them.
optionExplanations.A: Few-shot examples establish a clear pattern for format normalization without requiring external lookups or assumptions about real-time data.
optionExplanations.D: Outputting both raw and normalized values increases token cost and still requires deterministic post-processing, adding redundant steps.

questions.title: Long-Running Agent Session Context Bloat and Token Budget Pressure
questions.text: A customer support agent using multi-turn reasoning over a 10-hour shift accumulates ~400K tokens of conversation history (tickets, chat logs, tool results). Token budget alerts fire at 900K. The agent remains effective at responding to new customer queries, but latency is increasing. Which context management strategy best preserves response quality while staying within budget?

options.A: Implement context editing to selectively drop successful-resolution tool results (e.g., completed ticket confirmations) while preserving failed attempts and customer messages that inform reasoning.
options.B: Switch to summarization mode: every 20 turns, replace the oldest 50K tokens of conversation with a single-paragraph summary, keeping the full conversation in a side database for reference only.
options.C: Migrate to a shorter 200K context window model to force stricter conversation pruning and reduce per-request token overhead.
options.D: Enable automatic prompt caching on the system message and initial context, allowing recent turns (last 50K tokens) to stay in active context while older turns are compressed.

questions.explanation: Prompt caching preserves latency and token efficiency by caching the stable system message and initial context, while context editing (deleting resolved tool outputs) removes redundant information without losing reasoning history. This combination keeps response quality high while reducing active context pressure.

optionExplanations.D: This approach uses caching to avoid re-transmitting large stable blocks and keeps recent context in the window where the model needs it most for current reasoning.
optionExplanations.B: Summarization loses fine-grained detail and forces the model to reason about compressed information; it may miss nuances needed for accurate customer support decisions.
optionExplanations.A: Selective deletion of tool results is a good tactic but insufficient alone; pairing it with caching (A) is more effective at managing overall token budget and latency.
optionExplanations.C: Switching models forces users to accept lower reasoning depth and compatibility issues; it's a last resort only after context optimization has been exhausted.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Coordination with Conflicting Tool Outputs
questions.text: You are building a research orchestration system where Agent A (fact-checker) and Agent B (data analyst) both call a lookup_company_data tool with identical parameters but receive different company revenue figures (Agent A: $50M, Agent B: $55M). The agents cannot proceed until they agree on a single value. What is the most effective approach to resolve this conflict?

options.A: Introduce a third verifier agent that calls an authoritative data source tool and uses that result as the canonical value for both agents.
options.B: Have Agent A call the tool again to verify its original result, then accept whichever agent retrieved the data most recently.
options.C: Instruct both agents to return null for company_revenue and escalate the discrepancy to a human analyst.
options.D: Configure both agents to use the same prompt caching block for the lookup_company_data context so they retrieve identical results.

questions.explanation: Introducing a third agent with access to an authoritative source breaks the deadlock systematically without requiring human intervention or losing data. Agent-to-agent voting or re-querying does not address the root cause of data source inconsistency.

optionExplanations.B: Re-querying Agent A does not resolve the disagreement; if the tool returned different values, asking again risks the same or new inconsistency.
optionExplanations.A: A canonical verifier agent with authoritative source access provides a single source of truth that both downstream agents can trust.
optionExplanations.C: Escalating to humans for every discrepancy defeats automation and adds latency; the issue is resolvable programmatically.
optionExplanations.D: Prompt caching does not guarantee identical tool results; it optimizes latency and cost but does not change the underlying data source or API behavior.

questions.title: MCP Tool Definition with Ambiguous Input Constraints
questions.text: You are designing an MCP server that exposes a send_email tool. The tool must accept email addresses, but the spec allows both a single email (string) and multiple emails (array). During testing, different clients interpret the schema differently: some send {"recipient": "user@example.com"} while others send {"recipient": ["user@example.com", "admin@example.com"]}. How should you revise the MCP tool definition to eliminate this ambiguity?

options.A: Keep the ambiguous schema but add input validation logic on the server to coerce strings into single-element arrays before processing.
options.B: Add a oneOf constraint with separate string and array branches, each with clear examples in the description field.
options.C: Change the schema to accept only a single recipient (string) and require clients to call send_email multiple times for bulk distribution.
options.D: Define two separate tools: send_email_single and send_email_bulk, each with unambiguous input types.

questions.explanation: Separate tools with unambiguous contracts eliminate interpretation variance at schema level, making client implementations predictable and reducing server-side validation burden. Coercion (C) hides the ambiguity rather than solving it, and oneOf (A) still allows both patterns, inviting inconsistent client code.

optionExplanations.B: While oneOf clarifies intent, it still permits two different input patterns; clients may implement inconsistently, and server validation logic becomes complex.
optionExplanations.C: Forcing single recipients increases call overhead and may frustrate clients with legitimate bulk-send workflows; not user-friendly.
optionExplanations.A: Server-side coercion masks the ambiguity and makes debugging harder; clients remain uncertain about the true contract.
optionExplanations.D: Clear separation of concerns ensures each tool has a single, obvious input type, reducing client confusion and server-side edge cases.

questions.title: Preventing Hallucinated Product SKUs in Structured Extraction
questions.text: Your e-commerce system uses Claude to extract product information from supplier catalogs into a JSON schema {"name": string, "sku": string, "price": float}. During testing, the model frequently invents plausible SKUs for products mentioned in text that do not include an explicit SKU field (e.g., "Blue Widget" becomes "BWG-2024-001"). This causes downstream inventory errors. What is the best way to eliminate hallucinated SKUs?

options.A: Change the sku field to a nullable field and add explicit prompt instruction: 'Return null for sku unless the catalog text explicitly states an SKU code.'
options.B: Modify the schema to make sku required and add a validation regex that only accepts SKU patterns found in historical catalog data.
options.C: Add a post-processing step that cross-references extracted SKUs against a master database; reject any SKU not found in inventory.
options.D: Use a stricter model (e.g., upgrade to Claude 3.5 Sonnet) with better instruction-following to reduce hallucination.

questions.explanation: Explicit permission and instruction to return null directly addresses the root cause—the model currently assumes it should generate a plausible value rather than admit missing data. This is both the simplest and most reliable fix.

optionExplanations.A: Giving the model explicit permission and instruction to return null for missing values prevents hallucination at the source, eliminating downstream validation overhead.
optionExplanations.C: Post-hoc validation catches hallucinations but does not prevent them; it adds latency and still requires a fallback strategy when SKUs are invalid.
optionExplanations.D: Model upgrades may improve general instruction-following but do not guarantee elimination of this specific hallucination pattern; instruction clarity is the primary lever.
optionExplanations.B: Making sku required forces hallucination; the model will generate a value rather than fail validation, worsening the problem.

questions.title: Context Window Overflow in Long-Running Customer Support Agent
questions.text: Your Claude-based customer support agent has been running for 6 weeks, accumulating 45 conversation threads, each averaging 30 messages (chat history, tool call logs, customer notes). The total conversation context is now 280k tokens. Recently, the agent began responding with less relevant advice and took 2–3 seconds longer per message. API logs show that each request is hitting the 1M token context window limit. What is the most efficient approach to restore performance while preserving agent knowledge?

options.A: Implement automatic prompt caching on the system message and most recent 5 conversation threads, moving older threads to a file-based archive.
options.B: Upgrade to a model with a 2M token context window so the agent can retain all 45 threads without overflow.
options.C: Use the Compaction feature to automatically summarize older conversation segments when context approaches the window limit.
options.D: Manually review and delete threads older than 2 weeks to reduce context size, then implement token counting on new conversations.

questions.explanation: Compaction automatically summarizes older segments server-side when approaching the limit, preserving knowledge while maintaining efficiency without manual curation or re-architecture. Caching (A) helps but requires manual thread management; window upgrades (D) defer rather than solve the problem.

optionExplanations.A: Prompt caching reduces repeated computation but does not address the growing token count of active conversations; manual archive management is brittle.
optionExplanations.C: Compaction automatically manages context by summarizing older exchanges, maintaining long-term knowledge while keeping recent messages intact and token efficiency high.
optionExplanations.D: Deleting threads loses valuable context; token counting alone does not prevent overflow, only measures it.
optionExplanations.B: A larger window delays the problem; as the agent runs longer, context will grow indefinitely without automatic summarization or pruning.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration: Managing Tool Call Ordering
questions.text: You are building a research assistant that uses three agents: DataFetcher (retrieves raw datasets), Analyzer (processes and validates data), and Summarizer (creates reports). Currently, all agents run in parallel, but Analyzer frequently fails because it receives incomplete datasets from DataFetcher. What is the most effective orchestration pattern?

options.A: Implement parallel execution with a post-processing validation step that re-runs failed agents automatically.
options.B: Add a queue-based system where each agent polls for task readiness, allowing flexible scheduling without hard dependencies.
options.C: Upgrade to a more capable model for Analyzer to handle partial datasets gracefully and infer missing values.
options.D: Implement sequential orchestration where Analyzer waits for DataFetcher completion, then Summarizer waits for Analyzer completion before starting.

questions.explanation: Sequential orchestration with explicit dependencies ensures downstream agents receive complete, validated inputs, eliminating the root cause of Analyzer failures. Hard ordering prevents wasted retries and guarantees data integrity across the pipeline.

optionExplanations.A: Automatic retries on parallel execution will still fail if the underlying data is incomplete when Analyzer runs.
optionExplanations.B: Queue-based polling adds complexity and does not guarantee Analyzer receives complete data before execution starts.
optionExplanations.C: Upgrading the model does not solve the architectural problem of incomplete input data reaching Analyzer.
optionExplanations.D: This establishes clear dependency management where each stage completes before the next begins, ensuring data completeness.

questions.title: MCP Tool Schema Design: Preventing Hallucinated Parameters
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes a database_query tool that accepts a table_name (string) and optional filters (object). During testing, Claude sometimes invents filter field names not present in your schema definition, causing runtime errors. The tool executes but returns zero results or crashes when invalid filter keys are used. How should you redesign the tool definition?

options.A: Remove the optional filters parameter entirely and require callers to construct SQL WHERE clauses as raw strings.
options.B: Keep the current schema but add a second tool called validate_filter that Claude must call before using database_query.
options.C: Implement server-side validation that silently ignores unknown filter keys instead of raising errors.
options.D: Add enum constraints to the filters object schema listing all valid field names, and update the tool description to explicitly forbid unlisted fields.

questions.explanation: Enum constraints in the JSON schema provide Claude with explicit instruction on valid field names, eliminating the hallucination problem at the source. Combined with descriptive tool text, this is the standard MCP practice for constraining parameter space.

optionExplanations.A: Raw SQL strings reintroduce security risks and lose the benefits of structured schema validation.
optionExplanations.B: A separate validation tool adds unnecessary latency and still does not prevent the initial hallucination.
optionExplanations.C: Silent failures hide bugs and produce incorrect query results, degrading reliability.
optionExplanations.D: Schema enums directly instruct Claude which fields are valid, preventing invalid parameter generation.

questions.title: Structured Extraction: Balancing Schema Strictness with Null Handling
questions.text: Your customer support system extracts ticket metadata (priority, category, requester_email, resolution_time_hours) from support emails using structured output with a JSON schema. Evaluation reveals two problems: (1) priority is sometimes hallucinated as a plausible but unsupported value like "critical-urgent", and (2) resolution_time_hours is frequently set to null even when the email mentions an expected turnaround. What is the best fix?

options.A: Make all fields required and non-nullable in the schema, forcing the model to always generate a value.
options.B: Use a two-stage extraction where the first call identifies if data exists, and the second performs extraction only if confirmed.
options.C: Switch to a custom post-processing function that validates each extracted field against a whitelist and regenerates missing values.
options.D: Keep nullable fields but add enum constraints for priority and explicit prompt instructions to extract resolution_time_hours from turnaround language.

questions.explanation: Enum constraints eliminate hallucinated priority values, while targeted prompt instructions guide the model to recognize and extract turnaround language without forcing false nullability. This preserves data integrity where values exist and null precision where they do not.

optionExplanations.A: Requiring all fields forces hallucinated values when data is genuinely missing, introducing false positives.
optionExplanations.B: Two-stage extraction doubles token cost and latency without directly improving the extraction accuracy per call.
optionExplanations.C: Post-processing validation adds latency and cannot distinguish real nulls from missed extractions without a separate signal.
optionExplanations.D: Enums restrict priority to valid values and explicit instructions teach the model to detect and extract turnaround phrases.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Handoff During Customer Escalation
questions.text: Your customer support agent successfully resolves 80% of billing inquiries but encounters a complex dispute requiring legal review. The agent has gathered comprehensive context (account history, transaction details, customer communication log) spanning 45K tokens. When you route to a legal specialist agent, you must decide how to transfer context. Passing the full 45K tokens increases latency by 200ms; using a 5K summarized handoff note reduces latency but risks losing critical details the legal agent may need for compliance documentation.

options.A: Compress the context using lossy summarization and discard the original messages after handoff to reduce token consumption.
options.B: Route only the transaction ID and customer ID, requiring the legal agent to re-query the system independently to avoid context bloat.
options.C: Always pass the full 45K token context to ensure the legal agent has access to every detail for compliance purposes, accepting the latency cost.
options.D: Use the 5K summary and implement a callback mechanism so the legal agent can retrieve full context on-demand if needed, balancing latency and access.

questions.explanation: On-demand retrieval balances the need for compliance documentation (full context remains accessible) with system efficiency (no upfront latency penalty unless the legal agent actually needs details). This pattern supports reliable handoff without forcing unnecessary token overhead.

optionExplanations.A: Lossy summarization and discarding originals violates compliance requirements for legal disputes where original message content is often required as evidence.
optionExplanations.B: Re-querying forces the legal agent to rebuild context, duplicating work and introducing consistency risk if the system state changed during the handoff.
optionExplanations.C: Passing full context every time adds unnecessary latency to all escalations, even when the legal agent may only need summary-level information.
optionExplanations.D: This approach provides access to full context when needed while minimizing latency for the common case of summary-sufficient decisions.

questions.title: MCP Tool Resource Discovery During Dynamic Workflow
questions.text: You are designing an MCP server that exposes both static analysis tools (code_review, lint_check) and dynamic resources (project_config.json, environment_variables). A Claude Code agent needs to decide whether to call a tool or fetch a resource based on the user's request: 'Tell me the current deployment status and validate the Dockerfile.' The agent must know which operations require tool execution versus resource fetching to avoid unnecessary round-trips.

options.A: Require the agent to call a capabilities check tool on every request to dynamically determine if the requested operation exists before attempting execution.
options.B: Create a single orchestration tool that accepts both operation type and target name, centralizing all requests through one interface regardless of whether it is a tool or resource call.
options.C: Implement a tool discovery endpoint that returns a schema listing all available tools and resources, allowing the agent to query capabilities before executing any operation.
options.D: Define clear naming conventions (e.g., tools start with verb_object, resources use file paths) and document the MCP server interface so the agent can infer the correct operation type.

questions.explanation: Clear naming conventions and documentation enable the agent to infer operation type without extra round-trips, reducing latency while keeping the MCP protocol efficient. This approach leverages the agent's instruction-following ability rather than adding query overhead.

optionExplanations.A: Checking capabilities on every request defeats the purpose of MCP—the server should publish its schema once, not validate availability per-call.
optionExplanations.B: Centralizing through a single orchestration tool obscures the semantic difference between tools and resources, making the MCP interface less transparent.
optionExplanations.C: A discovery endpoint adds an extra call before every operation, increasing latency and complexity when the interface can be understood statically.
optionExplanations.D: Consistent naming patterns (tools as verbs, resources as paths) allow efficient routing without runtime discovery, matching MCP protocol design principles.

questions.title: Structured Output Hallucination in Real-Time Extraction
questions.text: Your system extracts structured metadata (speaker_name, timestamp, sentiment) from live customer support call transcripts using a JSON schema. During a 10-minute call, the model frequently generates plausible speaker names (e.g., 'Agent Sarah' or 'Customer Michael') that are never mentioned in the audio transcript. Stakeholders want all three fields populated for every extraction. You must reduce hallucination while meeting the requirement for complete structured output.

options.A: Post-process each extraction with a verification tool that searches the original transcript for each extracted value, replacing unverified speaker names with null.
options.B: Switch to a stricter JSON schema that makes speaker_name a required field with an enum constraint listing only speaker IDs detected in the previous turn.
options.C: Modify the prompt to explicitly state: 'Extract only speaker_name if explicitly mentioned in the transcript; otherwise return null for that field. Timestamp and sentiment must always be present.'
options.D: Upgrade to a larger model and use a few-shot prompt with examples showing null values for missing speaker names, training the model to avoid hallucination.

questions.explanation: Explicit prompt instructions that permit null values directly address the root cause—the model hallucinating to satisfy an perceived requirement for complete data. This lightweight approach is faster and more reliable than post-processing or schema constraints.

optionExplanations.A: Post-processing verification adds latency and complexity; the model should be instructed correctly upfront rather than corrected after generation.
optionExplanations.B: Constraining speaker_name to an enum learned in the previous turn is brittle and prevents the model from correctly identifying new speakers mid-conversation.
optionExplanations.C: Telling the model exactly when to return null removes the pressure to hallucinate plausible values and directly solves the extraction quality problem.
optionExplanations.D: Model upgrades and few-shot examples help but add cost and complexity; explicit null-permission instructions are the direct, cheaper solution.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Orchestration: Handling Partial Tool Failures
questions.text: You are building a research agent that coordinates three sub-agents: a web_search agent, a document_analyzer agent, and a synthesis agent. During a complex query, web_search times out after 30 seconds, but document_analyzer and synthesis agents complete successfully with partial data. The parent orchestrator detects the timeout and must decide how to proceed. What is the most effective strategy?

options.A: Implement a fallback that replaces web_search with a cached result from a previous similar query, even if the cache is older than ideal.
options.B: Abandon the request entirely and ask the user to resubmit with a simpler query that is less likely to trigger timeouts.
options.C: Allow synthesis to proceed with available data from document_analyzer, transparently note the web_search limitation in the response, and offer the user an option to retry or refine the query.
options.D: Immediately retry web_search with exponential backoff until it succeeds, blocking the synthesis agent from proceeding until all data sources are complete.

questions.explanation: This approach maximizes value with available information, maintains transparency about limitations, and preserves user agency. Blocking on retries (A) risks poor user experience; abandoning entirely (C) wastes partial results; stale fallbacks (D) reduce accuracy without the user knowing.

optionExplanations.A: Serving cached data without disclosure risks presenting outdated information as current research.
optionExplanations.B: Rejecting the request discards valuable work already done by two agents and shifts burden to the user unnecessarily.
optionExplanations.C: Completing work with partial data and clear communication about limitations provides the best balance of utility and transparency.
optionExplanations.D: Blocking the entire orchestration on a single agent timeout defeats the purpose of distributed agents and frustrates users with delays.

questions.title: MCP Server Design: Resource Scoping and Query Complexity Limits
questions.text: You are designing an MCP server that exposes a company knowledge base with 500,000+ documents. Early testing shows that unscoped resource queries can return megabytes of data, causing client timeouts and excessive bandwidth usage. A partner is planning to integrate your MCP server into their Claude instance serving 10,000 concurrent users. What is the most robust server-side mitigation?

options.A: Implement client-side rate limiting using API keys, allowing high-trust partners to request higher limits and monitoring usage post-deployment.
options.B: Disable resource queries entirely and only expose tools that require explicit parameter specification to prevent accidental data floods.
options.C: Document best practices for clients and trust that partner implementations will configure appropriate usage limits on their side.
options.D: Implement mandatory query filtering, result pagination with a maximum of 50 documents per response, and document size caps to ensure predictable resource consumption.

questions.explanation: Server-side enforcement of pagination, filtering, and size caps protects all clients automatically regardless of implementation quality. Relying on client discipline (B) is fragile at scale; disabling resources (C) reduces utility; client-side rate limiting (D) provides no protection against accidental large queries before they consume bandwidth.

optionExplanations.A: Rate limiting after bandwidth is consumed is reactive and ineffective against a single massive query that exhausts limits immediately.
optionExplanations.B: Disabling queries sacrifices the primary value proposition of the MCP server to prevent a solvable problem.
optionExplanations.C: Documentation alone cannot enforce limits and leaves the service vulnerable to poorly configured or malicious client implementations.
optionExplanations.D: Server-side constraints prevent runaway queries from any client and ensure consistent, predictable resource usage across all integrations.

questions.title: Structured Output Hallucination: Handling Ambiguous Source Data
questions.text: Your extraction pipeline uses a JSON schema to parse customer support tickets into structured fields (ticket_id, customer_name, issue_category, resolution_status). You observe that when tickets contain informal language or typos, the model frequently infers plausible but unverified values for resolution_status—often labeling tickets as "resolved" when the actual ticket says "waiting for customer approval" or "pending technical fix." What is the most direct solution to reduce hallucination?

options.A: Add explicit instructions to the extraction prompt that resolution_status must be extracted only from explicit keywords like "resolved," "open," or "closed," with a fallback to "unknown" for ambiguous cases.
options.B: Implement a post-processing validation step using a second LLM call to fact-check each resolution_status field against the original ticket text.
options.C: Make resolution_status a required field and apply strict schema validation to reject any response that contains an unrecognized status value.
options.D: Switch to a larger, more capable model that has better instruction-following and reduced hallucination tendencies.

questions.explanation: Explicit prompt guidance directly addresses the root cause by instructing the model when to return null or "unknown" instead of inferring plausible values. Upgrading the model (A) is expensive and doesn't guarantee the behavior; post-processing validation (C) adds latency and cost; strict validation (D) forces hallucinated values into the schema without preventing them.

optionExplanations.A: Clear guidance on allowed values and explicit permission to return unknown when ambiguous directly prevents plausible inference.
optionExplanations.B: Validation after the fact consumes extra tokens and cannot prevent the original hallucination, only detect it.
optionExplanations.C: Schema-level validation prevents invalid values but does not prevent the model from hallucinating a value that matches a recognized category.
optionExplanations.D: A larger model may improve instruction-following but does not directly address the instruction to avoid hallucinating missing information.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Coordination: Handling Concurrent Tool Failures
questions.text: You're building a research agent that orchestrates three sub-agents: one retrieves academic papers via API, another extracts citations, and a third validates source URLs. During a large literature review, the citation extraction agent times out on 40% of papers while the URL validator succeeds consistently. The research agent has partial results and must decide how to proceed without blocking the entire workflow. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Continue with successfully validated papers, gracefully handle partial citation data, and queue failed extractions for asynchronous retry without blocking downstream analysis.
options.B: Halt all processing and retry all three agents together until every citation is extracted and validated, treating the workflow as atomic.
options.C: Escalate timeout errors to a human reviewer who manually extracts citations for the 40% of failing cases.
options.D: Switch to a simpler single-agent model that processes papers sequentially to avoid coordination overhead and timeout issues.

questions.explanation: This approach maximizes value from available results while maintaining resilience; partial data enables progress, asynchronous retry prevents blocking, and the workflow remains transparent to the user. Option B balances efficiency and robustness without sacrificing automation or data quality.

optionExplanations.A: Graceful degradation with async retry is the correct orchestration pattern for multi-agent systems with variable latency.
optionExplanations.B: Treating the workflow as atomic blocks progress unnecessarily when partial results are useful; timeouts are common and should not force complete re-execution.
optionExplanations.C: Manual escalation reduces automation value and is not justified when the system can proceed with partial data and retry failures.
optionExplanations.D: Sequential processing eliminates parallelism gains and does not address the underlying timeout root cause.

questions.title: Customer Intent Classification: Distinguishing Implicit vs. Explicit Refund Requests
questions.text: Your customer support chatbot uses a JSON schema to classify incoming messages into intents (refund_request, complaint, status_inquiry, etc.) with confidence scores. During testing, you observe the model frequently classifies messages like "I received the wrong item and I'm frustrated" as complaint with 0.92 confidence instead of refund_request with 0.88 confidence. The messages contain refund-eligible keywords but lack the word "refund." How should you adjust the prompt to improve classification accuracy?

options.A: Upgrade to a more capable model to improve semantic understanding of implicit intent without changing the prompt.
options.B: Include in the system prompt explicit instructions that messages indicating product issues (damage, wrong item, missing parts) should be classified as refund_request even without the explicit word refund, along with examples showing this reasoning.
options.C: Require customers to explicitly state refund requests and retrain the chatbot to reject implicit requests and ask clarifying questions.
options.D: Add a post-processing rule that checks for refund-eligibility keywords (wrong item, damaged, missing) and overrides the intent if confidence is within 0.05 of the top prediction.

questions.explanation: Explicit prompt instructions with concrete examples teach the model the correct classification rule for implicit refund intent, directly addressing the root cause of the misclassification without post-processing overhead or capability upgrades.

optionExplanations.A: Model capability upgrades are not the root cause; the current model can handle this task with clearer instructions.
optionExplanations.B: Prompt instructions with examples establish the correct semantic rule for implicit refund detection, leveraging the model's existing capability.
optionExplanations.C: Requiring explicit refund language degrades user experience and eliminates the automation value of intent classification.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing rules are brittle and do not generalize; they treat the symptom rather than teaching the model the underlying classification logic.

questions.title: MCP Tool Output Serialization: Handling Binary and Structured Responses
questions.text: You're designing an MCP server that exposes a document processing tool returning both structured metadata (JSON) and binary content (PDF bytes). The client expects a single JSON response. Currently, the tool returns PDF bytes as base64-encoded strings, which inflates payload size by 33% and slows down MCP message parsing for metadata-only requests. What is the most effective design improvement?

options.A: Compress the base64-encoded PDF with gzip and embed it in the JSON response to reduce size while maintaining a single tool interface.
options.B: Implement a resource-based approach where the tool returns metadata with a resource URI reference, and the client fetches the PDF separately using MCP resources protocol.
options.C: Keep a single tool but restructure the response schema to make PDF content optional and nullable, returning only metadata by default unless explicitly requested.
options.D: Split the tool into two separate MCP tools: one returns JSON metadata only, the other returns base64-encoded PDF; the client chooses which to call.

questions.explanation: The MCP resource protocol is designed precisely for this use case: separating metadata from large binary assets, reducing default payload size, and allowing clients to fetch binary content only when needed. This follows MCP's three-primitive design (tools, resources, prompts).

optionExplanations.A: Compression adds computational overhead and still embeds binary data in JSON, missing the separation of concerns that resources provide.
optionExplanations.B: Resources are the MCP-native abstraction for large binary or secondary content, enabling efficient metadata-first retrieval.
optionExplanations.C: Making content optional is a partial solution but does not leverage MCP's native resource abstraction designed for this pattern.
optionExplanations.D: Two separate tools increase surface area and force the client to decide upfront which to call, reducing flexibility.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Handoff: Customer Service to Billing Resolution
questions.text: You are building a customer service orchestration system where a support agent collects initial complaint data, then must hand off to a billing specialist agent with all relevant context. The support agent gathers customer ID, order history, and complaint type, but the handoff frequently loses critical details like shipping address or payment method. What is the most effective approach to preserve context fidelity across agent boundaries?

options.A: Include the entire conversation history and explicitly surface extraction metadata in the handoff message so the billing agent has full visibility into what was already confirmed.
options.B: Store all extracted fields in a shared database and pass only the customer ID to the next agent, requiring the billing agent to re-fetch needed data.
options.C: Use a prompt instruction in the billing agent to ask the customer to repeat key details to ensure accuracy despite potential context loss.
options.D: Implement a structured context object containing both raw conversation snippets and normalized fields, with explicit validation that required billing fields are present before handoff.

questions.explanation: Structured handoff objects with validation gates ensure no critical fields are lost while remaining efficient; raw snippets preserve nuance, normalized fields enable direct action, and explicit validation prevents silent failures.

optionExplanations.A: Full history visibility alone does not guarantee billing agent will identify which fields are relevant or validated, leading to re-confirmation inefficiency.
optionExplanations.B: Database-only passing creates latency and requires the billing agent to infer context from minimal data, risking repeated customer questions.
optionExplanations.C: Re-asking the customer for information already collected erodes trust and increases conversation length without technical benefit.
optionExplanations.D: This combines preservation of context (history plus structured data), validation (gate before handoff), and agent efficiency (pre-normalized fields ready to act on).

questions.title: Tool Schema Evolution: Breaking Change in External API Response Structure
questions.text: Your MCP tool wraps an external payment processor API. Version 1.0 returns `{status: string, transaction_id: string}`, but the provider announces v2.0 will return `{result: {status: string, txn_id: string}, metadata: {timestamp, processor}}`. Your agent is live in production and calls this tool 50+ times daily. How should you handle the schema transition to avoid breaking agent behavior?

options.A: Maintain a local cache of v1.0 responses and only call v2.0 API when the cache misses to ensure backward compatibility indefinitely.
options.B: Create a new tool (pay_v2) alongside the old one and update agent instructions to prefer pay_v2, deprecating the old tool after one week.
options.C: Detect the API version at runtime, normalize both v1.0 and v2.0 responses to a consistent internal schema, and update your tool definition to reflect the canonical output.
options.D: Immediately update your tool schema to match v2.0 and rely on Claude to adapt its parsing logic automatically based on the new structure.

questions.explanation: Runtime detection with response normalization decouples your tool schema from provider churn, ensures the agent always sees a stable interface, and allows graceful migration without duplicate tools or agent prompt hacks.

optionExplanations.A: Caching indefinitely delays the real migration, consumes storage, and does not solve the problem of eventually needing to handle v2.0-only features.
optionExplanations.B: Dual tools create cognitive load for agent prompting, require manual deprecation coordination, and do not scale if the provider iterates further.
optionExplanations.C: This pattern shields the agent from provider volatility, maintains a single source of truth in your tool definition, and allows transparent API version management.
optionExplanations.D: Changing schema without normalization breaks existing agent behavior since Claude has no way to know the structure changed and adapt parsing on the fly.

questions.title: Structured Extraction with Confidence Scores: Balancing Precision and Completeness
questions.text: You are extracting legal contract metadata (party names, signature dates, liability caps, renewal terms) from PDFs using a JSON schema with all fields marked required. During evaluation, Claude sometimes returns correct data but marks confidence below 0.7 when information is genuinely present but ambiguous (e.g., liability cap buried in a subsection). Users complain that 15% of extractions are rejected due to low confidence even though the data is accurate. What is the best approach to improve usability without sacrificing extraction reliability?

options.A: Implement a two-stage extraction pipeline: first extract with confidence scores, then for low-confidence fields, run a targeted re-extraction with a specialized prompt asking Claude to justify its confidence threshold.
options.B: Remove the confidence score requirement and mark all fields as nullable, allowing Claude to return null for any field it is uncertain about regardless of source clarity.
options.C: Add a supplementary prompt instruction asking Claude to return not just confidence scores but also the page number and exact quote span where each field was found, then allow humans to validate high-uncertainty results.
options.D: Increase the model tier to a more capable model and lower the confidence threshold to 0.5 to accept more results, trading some false positives for higher recall.

questions.explanation: Providing source citations (page and quote) gives users the ability to validate low-confidence extractions themselves, maintaining reliability while reducing false rejections and building appropriate trust in ambiguous cases.

optionExplanations.A: A second extraction pass doubles latency and token cost for marginal confidence improvement without adding evidence for users to trust.
optionExplanations.B: Removing confidence entirely loses signal about data quality; nullable fields do not help users distinguish between missing data and uncertain data.
optionExplanations.C: Citations empower human-in-the-loop review without changing extraction logic, preserving accuracy while making low-confidence cases actionable for users.
optionExplanations.D: Lowering thresholds blindly increases false positives; model tier alone does not guarantee better confidence calibration on ambiguous source material.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Coordination: Conflicting Tool Outputs from Parallel Agents
questions.text: You are orchestrating a research workflow with two parallel agents: one fetches academic papers via search_papers, the other retrieves citation counts via analytics_service. Both tools return successfully, but the citation count for a key paper differs by 15% between sources. The first agent proposes citing the higher count, the second agent flags the discrepancy as a reliability issue. How should your orchestration layer handle this conflict?

options.A: Use the higher citation count since both agents successfully executed their tools and more citations indicate greater impact.
options.B: Implement a consensus check that queries a third authoritative source, logs the discrepancy, and returns the verified count or escalates if sources remain inconsistent.
options.C: Halt the workflow and require manual human review before proceeding, since tool outputs contradict each other.
options.D: Average the two counts to arrive at a neutral middle value that neither agent disputes.

questions.explanation: Multi-agent orchestration must validate conflicting data through authoritative verification or escalation logic, not average or ignore discrepancies. This ensures output reliability and traces the source of truth.

optionExplanations.A: Accepting higher values without verification introduces systematic bias and masks data quality issues.
optionExplanations.B: Adding a verification step creates defensive data validation that improves confidence and provides audit trails.
optionExplanations.C: Halting all workflow for minor discrepancies is inefficient; graduated escalation (verify, flag, then escalate) is better.
optionExplanations.D: Averaging unreliable data does not resolve the underlying conflict and masks which source is correct.

questions.title: MCP Tool Definition: Missing Fallback for External Service Downtime
questions.text: You are defining an MCP tool that calls a third-party payment gateway to validate credit card transactions. The tool schema includes required parameters (card_number, expiry, cvv, amount) and a description stating it validates cards. During integration testing, the payment gateway experiences a 15-minute outage. Your tool definition has no fallback or timeout strategy. What is the best way to handle this in your MCP tool design?

options.A: Remove the tool from production until the payment gateway guarantees 99.99% uptime, since external services are inherently unreliable.
options.B: Add a boolean parameter force_offline_mode that triggers a mock validation response when the real service is unavailable.
options.C: Add a timeout parameter to the tool schema and implement retry logic with exponential backoff; document the SLA expectation and recommended timeout values in the tool description.
options.D: Modify the tool to always return a cached validation result from the previous successful call for any request made during downtime.

questions.explanation: Robust MCP tools must declare timeouts and retry strategies in their schema and documentation, allowing clients to set expectations and handle transient failures gracefully without introducing cache staleness or mock data.

optionExplanations.A: No external service guarantees perfect uptime; resilience design is required, not avoidance.
optionExplanations.B: Mock validation modes bypass security checks and create uncontrolled liability if used in production decisions.
optionExplanations.C: Explicit timeout and retry configuration gives clients control and visibility into tool behavior during degradation.
optionExplanations.D: Cached results can be stale and masked as current, violating the tool's contract and creating security risks.

questions.title: Structured Output Hallucination: Model Invents Metadata Not in Source
questions.text: Your document ingestion pipeline extracts structured metadata (title, author, publication_date, keywords, page_count) from PDFs using a JSON schema with all fields nullable. During QA, you notice the model frequently generates plausible but unverifiable author names and publication dates for technical documents where these fields are not explicitly stated. The model appears to be inferring based on content tone. How should you address this?

options.A: Switch to a smaller model that is more conservative and less likely to infer missing information.
options.B: Implement post-processing that compares extracted fields against the original text and rejects the entire extraction if any field cannot be directly cited.
options.C: Add explicit instructions in the system prompt: "Return null for any field where information is not directly visible or stated in the source document. Do not infer or estimate missing fields."
options.D: Make all fields required (non-nullable) and wrap the extraction in a second pass that validates each field exists in the source text.

questions.explanation: Explicit null-return instructions address the root cause by giving the model clear permission and expectation to return null rather than hallucinate plausible values, reducing false positives without adding latency.

optionExplanations.A: Model size is not the root cause; the model needs explicit instruction, not degradation.
optionExplanations.B: Post-processing validation catches hallucinations but adds cost and latency; prevention via instruction is more efficient.
optionExplanations.C: Direct prompt instruction prevents hallucination by making null-return the expected behavior for missing data.
optionExplanations.D: Required fields force the model to hallucinate rather than return null, worsening the problem.

questions.title: Multi-Agent Research Coordination with Divergent Tool Outputs
questions.text: You are building a multi-agent research system where three specialized agents (literature_search, data_analysis, and trend_forecasting) run in parallel. The literature_search agent retrieves 50 papers but formats citations as plain text, while data_analysis expects structured JSON references. The trend_forecasting agent has already completed its work and is waiting. How should you coordinate the output handoff to maximize efficiency while ensuring data compatibility?

options.A: Implement a lightweight adapter layer that transforms literature_search output to JSON on-the-fly, allowing data_analysis and trend_forecasting to proceed without delay
options.B: Block all three agents until literature_search reformats all citations to JSON, then sequence them serially to guarantee format matching
options.C: Skip the literature_search results entirely and have data_analysis work only with trend_forecasting outputs to avoid format conflicts
options.D: Have trend_forecasting agent wait indefinitely; use a human reviewer to manually verify citation formats before data_analysis consumes the data

questions.explanation: A transformation adapter unblocks parallelism and eliminates manual bottlenecks while ensuring all downstream agents receive properly formatted input, balancing throughput against data integrity.

optionExplanations.A: This preserves concurrency, handles format translation transparently, and keeps all agents productive without manual intervention.
optionExplanations.B: Serial execution sacrifices the parallelism advantage and introduces unnecessary latency when format bridging is more efficient.
optionExplanations.C: Discarding valid data sources degrades research quality and violates the principle of leveraging all available agent outputs.
optionExplanations.D: Blocking trend_forecasting wastes compute and introduces human latency when automated format conversion is feasible.

questions.title: MCP Server Tool Namespace Collision During Integration
questions.text: Your team is integrating two Model Context Protocol servers: one from a database vendor (providing a tool called query_data) and one from an analytics platform (also providing a tool called query_data but with different parameters and behavior). Both servers are essential to your workflow. When you initialize the MCP client, both tools are registered but under the same name. How should you resolve this collision?

options.A: Keep only the first-registered tool and document for users that the second tool is unavailable
options.B: Modify one of the MCP server implementations to rename its tool to query_data_v2 before deployment
options.C: Implement a custom router in your agent code that detects query_data calls and decides which server handles each request based on context inspection
options.D: Use the MCP connector to alias one tool namespace with a prefix, mapping analytics.query_data and database.query_data as distinct tool names in the client

questions.explanation: MCP namespace prefixing via the connector allows both tools to coexist under distinct identifiable names, preserving functionality from both servers without requiring source code modifications or custom routing logic.

optionExplanations.A: Discarding one tool set reduces system capability and violates the requirement that both servers are essential.
optionExplanations.B: Modifying external vendor tool names upstream is invasive, creates maintenance burden, and violates separation of concerns.
optionExplanations.C: Runtime context-based routing is fragile, adds agent-level complexity, and obscures tool availability to the model.
optionExplanations.D: This leverages MCP's native namespace aliasing to keep tools distinct and discoverable while preserving both integrations cleanly.

questions.title: Structured Output Hallucination in Multi-Field Extraction
questions.text: Your customer support system extracts support ticket metadata (severity, category, customer_account_id, resolution_time_minutes) from email text using a required JSON schema. During testing, the model consistently generates plausible but unverified values for resolution_time_minutes when the email contains no explicit time information. Tickets are routed based on these extracted values, and incorrect times cause misrouting to SLA violation teams. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Make resolution_time_minutes a required field and implement strict regex validation to accept only email-matched numeric patterns
options.B: Switch to a more capable model with better instruction-following to reduce hallucination on numeric fields
options.C: Add explicit instructions to return null for resolution_time_minutes if no time reference appears in the email text, and make the field nullable
options.D: Post-process all extractions with a verification step that searches the source email for exact matches to each extracted value

questions.explanation: Giving the model explicit permission and instruction to return null directly addresses the hallucination root cause—the model fills gaps with plausible values when it should instead signal missing data.

optionExplanations.A: Making the field required does not prevent hallucination; it only guarantees a hallucinated value will be accepted if validation fails.
optionExplanations.B: A more capable model may reduce hallucination marginally but does not fix the underlying instruction gap that allows the model to invent values.
optionExplanations.C: This removes the implicit pressure to generate a value and allows the model to correctly signal absent information without hallucinating.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing verification is costly and does not prevent the original extraction error from propagating to routing logic.

questions.title: Agent Loops Back to Same Tool After Timeout
questions.text: Your customer support agent successfully uses lookup_customer to retrieve account details, but when it attempts to call process_refund, the tool times out after 8 seconds. The agent then immediately calls lookup_customer again with identical parameters.
